title: Deckard capacity verification & fallback plan
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 14 Aug 2029 09:23:00 -0700
Subject: Deckard capacity verification & fallback plan

Team —

Deckard (late-stage POC) is asking for a reserved compute corridor for the POC and first-quarter ramp: estimate provided is ~40 A100-equivalent GPUs sustained across a 60-day window (target POC window 2029-10-15 to 2029-12-15). Attached: deckard_mou_summary.pdf and workload profile.

Asking infra + ops to confirm:
- Can we provision a provisional reservation for that window?
- If not, what realistic fallback capacity (region/rate/model) can we offer?
- Recommended soft-hold workflow and TTL for the hold (how long can we keep a provisional slot before it must be released)

Pls reply with a quick snapshot by EOD today. This is a high-priority commercial ask; sales owners (Marcus) and CS (Grace) are cc'd.

Thanks,
Aditya
Aditya Rao
Head of Sales Strategy | Redwood Inference

Attachments: deckard_mou_summary.pdf (summary), deckard_workload_profile.xlsx (workload profile)


From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 14 Aug 2029 10:47:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Deckard capacity verification & fallback plan

Aditya —

Quick capacity snapshot (real numbers pulled at 10:30 PT):
- Total available A100-equivalent pools (US regions): 120 GPUs free across pools; 48 GPUs currently in soft-reserve for other late-stage deals (release windows vary).
- Regional breakdown: us-west: 52 free (20 soft-reserved), us-east: 40 free (18 soft-reserved), us-central: 28 free (10 soft-reserved).

Proposed options:
1) Soft-hold (preferred): place a provisional reservation for Deckard for 40 GPUs for 2029-10-15 -> 2029-12-15 with a 30-day TTL that requires a signed MOU or deposit to convert to a hard hold. Operational impact: holds reduce burst capacity for other deals; estimated cost of reservation (opportunity) documented in attachment.
2) Alternative (capacity-constrained): offer 24 GPUs of full A100-equivalent + automated model quantization pack that reduces compute need by ~35% (profiling based on the attached workload). Will require model engineering to validate QAT/INT8 suitability.
3) Multi-region hybrid: 20 GPUs in us-west + 20 GPUs in us-east with cross-region routing and prefix cache warming — slightly higher latency but isolates single-region outage risk.

Immediate next steps I recommend:
- Approve soft-hold workflow and TTL (owner: Monica)
- Request Deckard to confirm POC window and sign MOU to convert to hard hold (owner: Aditya)
- Engage ML infra team to evaluate quantization path (owner: Marcus)

Attachment: capacity_snapshot_20290814.csv (raw inventory export). Happy to run a deeper simulation if you want specific hours/day profiles.

—Rishi
Rishi Malhotra
Director, Infrastructure Capacity | Redwood Inference


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: Emily Park <emily@deckard.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 14 Aug 2029 13:02:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Deckard capacity verification & fallback plan

Emily —

Forwarding Rishi's capacity note. Summary: we can provision a provisional soft-hold for the requested window (2029-10-15 -> 2029-12-15) for 40 GPUs, pending an MOU/signature or deposit within a 30-day TTL. If you can't commit to full reservation we also have two fallback options: a quantized model pack to reduce GPU needs or a multi-region hybrid setup.

Please confirm:
- Final POC dates (if the 10/15 to 12/15 window is still accurate)
- Will your procurement team be able to sign the MOU or provide an initial deposit within 30 days?

I attached our provisional hold request for signature. Link to DocuSign is in the template — if procurement needs a PO number or specialized terms, loop them in and we can expedite.

Thanks — we want to make sure Deckard's POC has stable infra as you ramp.

Aditya Rao
Head of Sales Strategy | Redwood Inference

Attachments: provisional_hold_request.pdf

From: Emily Park <emily@deckard.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@deckard.ai
Date: Wed, 14 Aug 2029 15:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Deckard capacity verification & fallback plan

Aditya / Team —

Thanks for the quick turnaround and the transparency on options. Confirming: Deckard intends to proceed with the 2029-10-15 -> 2029-12-15 POC window and we want to prioritize the soft-hold to avoid capacity risk. Our procurement lead is on cc — they'll start review of the MOU and aim to return a signed copy or deposit within the 30-day TTL. Deal reference is DEAL-2029-045.

A couple of asks from our side:
- Can Redwood provide a simple SLA addendum for reserved compute (uptime target + failover behavior) we can attach to the MOU?
- If we need to invoke the multi-region fallback, what latency delta should we expect (ballpark ms)?

I'll upload a signed MOU draft this afternoon (provisionally signed by our BD).

Best,
Emily Park
Head of Partnerships | Deckard AI

Attachment: deckard_signed_mou.pdf (uploaded, signing in process)


From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Aug 2029 16:42:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Deckard capacity verification & fallback plan — next steps

Quick wrap:
- Deckard confirmed intent to proceed with the 10/15–12/15 window and procurement is reviewing the MOU.
- Rishi: please provision a provisional soft-hold ticket for 40 GPUs (soft-hold TTL 30 days) and mark the ticket high-priority. Include multi-region routing as an alternate path in the ticket notes.
- Marcus: coordinate with ML eng to scope quantization validation (estimate 2–4 days of effort to baseline).
- Grace: schedule a 30-minute capacity sync with Deckard for Fri 2029-08-16 11:00 PT — Calendar invite + Zoom link to be shared.

Related links: HubSpot deal (https://hubspot.redwood.com/deals/DEAL-2029-045), Drive folder for artefacts (https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1DeckardPOCProfile), DocuSign template (https://www.docusign.com/EXAMPLE/Deckard_MOU).

I'll raise the soft-hold in the capacity system now and add the ticket id to the thread once created.

Monica Patel

title: Intermittent throughput collapse — firewall sessions & DNS cache storms (BrightRoot)
preview:
From: ops@brightroot.com
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Jun 2029 08:12:00 -0700
Subject: High 5xx rate and throughput collapse across eu-us peering

Avery —

We're seeing a sharp increase in 5xxs and a 60-80% throughput drop starting ~07:55 PDT. Traces point at the VPC peering path to your eu-west transit (AS4455) where packets are being retransmitted and responses delayed. We also saw DNS timeouts where our resolvers report TTLs of 1s and a flood of A queries.

Attached: broot-debug.zip (pcap + traces + app logs)

Immediate impact: checkout flow failures for ~30% of users in US-east. We're hearing from product that this is hitting checkout conversions and potentially crossing our SLA.

Can you confirm whether any firewall rule/action, peering change, or DNS config rollouts happened in the last hour? We can provide packet captures on request.

- BrightRoot SRE (ops@brightroot.com)

From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>
To: ops@brightroot.com
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Jun 2029 09:05:22 -0700
Subject: Re: High 5xx rate and throughput collapse across eu-us peering

Thanks — we're on it.

I've opened internal incident INC-20290603-5472 and looped Karthik and Marcus. Initial check of our control plane events shows no intentional peering change. We'll start with firewall session tables and DNS resolver health checks.

Can you confirm the best time window for a coordinated packet capture? Also please leave the broot-debug.zip on the ticket; we'll pull it into the incident timeline.

> Avery —
>
> We're seeing a sharp increase in 5xxs and a 60-80% throughput drop starting ~07:55 PDT. Traces point at the VPC peering path to your eu-west transit (AS4455) where packets are being retransmitted and responses delayed. We also saw DNS timeouts where our resolvers report TTLs of 1s and a flood of A queries.

>
> Immediate impact: checkout flow failures for ~30% of users in US-east. We're hearing from product that this is hitting checkout conversions and potentially crossing our SLA.
>
> Can you confirm whether any firewall rule/action, peering change, or DNS config rollouts happened in the last hour? We can provide packet captures on request.
>
> - BrightRoot SRE (ops@brightroot.com)

-- 
Avery Johnson
Head of Sales Engineering
Redwood Inference
avery.johnson@redwood.com
+1 415 555 0121


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Jun 2029 11:20:10 -0700
Subject: FW: Quick findings — firewall session exhaustion + DNS resolver storm

Avery/Vanessa —

Quick findings from initial triage (summary):

- Firewall session table on edge-gw-3 is at 98% capacity (SYN/half-open spikes) during the incident window. Evidence: fw-session-stats.csv attached.
- Upstream DNS cluster returned misconfigured recursion behavior: one resolver flipped to authoritative-only mode under load, causing client resolvers to retry rapidly; effective TTL dropped to 1s in our logs.
- Netflows show many short-lived TCP connections with small payloads (likely health-check/keepalive churn from a partner). That churn is filling session tables faster than we can age entries.

Short-term mitigations applied (11:05 PDT):
- Increased session table size on edge-gw-3 (+30%) and tuned SYN backlog parameters.
- Temporarily rate-limited external client-to-edge SYNs at the T0 egress (policy applied to suspect AS ranges).
- Added a passive DNS cache eviction throttle and failed over BrightRoot DNS to secondary resolvers.

Remaining actions (urgent):
1) Coordinate with BrightRoot to identify any partner that started aggressive keepalives in that window; they may need to patch.
2) Plan a peering policy change to prefer alternate transit path during heavy SYN storms.
3) Deeper postmortem on why a resolver flipped to non-recursive mode; likely config drift.

I'll join the incident bridge in 15 mins. Attaching fw-session-stats.csv and a quick synthetic traceroute.

-Karthik

On Mon, Jun 3, 2029 at 09:05 AM Avery Johnson wrote:
> Thanks — we're on it.

Attachments: fw-session-stats.csv

From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 4 Jun 2029 07:30:00 -0700
Subject: Escalation to Execs: BrightRoot outage risk — potential SLA credit

Marcus/Avery —

Summary: BrightRoot (large retail customer) experienced a throughput/5xx incident affecting ~30% of checkouts over a ~40 minute period on 2029-06-03. Root cause is currently a compound of firewall session exhaustion + DNS resolver misbehavior. We've applied mitigations (session table increase, DNS failover, rate limits) but there's risk of recurrence until config & peering policy changes are implemented.

Impact: Potential revenue and an SLA breach given negotiated 99.9% availability for checkout paths. Customer is requesting an executive touchpoint and written incident timeline.

Recommendation & ask:
- Approve: immediate allocation of extra Dedicated capacity in us-east for BrightRoot to temporarily absorb spikes (one-time charge waived if Marcus approves).
- Approve: outreach from Sales leadership to offer a credit if the postmortem shows platform contribution.

Marcus — please confirm if you approve the credit waiver and temporary Dedicated burst (I will notify BrightRoot with a mitigation plan once approved).

-Vanessa

Vanessa Ortiz
Director, Customer Success
Redwood Inference
vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: ops@brightroot.com, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 5 Jun 2029 17:42:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Intermittent throughput collapse — firewall sessions & DNS cache storms (BrightRoot)

BrightRoot team,

Thank you for the quick signal and patience while we investigated. Summary of status:

- What happened: a combination of aggressive short-lived connections from a third-party partner and an internal DNS resolver config regression led to extremely low effective TTLs and a flood of queries; this increased connection churn which in turn exhausted the firewall session table on edge-gw-3. That combination produced elevated latency and 5xxs for your checkout traffic.
- What we did: increased session table, tuned SYN backlogs, failed over DNS, applied temporary SYN-rate limits, and provisioned Dedicated burst capacity in us-east while we harden rules.
- Outcome: traffic normalized by 2029-06-03 12:10 PDT. No further degradation reported; we will keep monitoring for 72 hours.

Next steps:
1) We'll run a joint review with BrightRoot next week (propose Thu 2029-06-13 10:00 PDT) — please let us know availability.
2) We'll deliver a full postmortem draft and remedial action plan by EOD 2029-06-07 (attached draft: postmortem-draft.md).
3) We will propose peering policy changes and a resolver config guardrail to prevent regression.

Marcus Lin
VP, Platform
Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.com

Date: Mon, Jun 3, 2029 at 11:20 AM

Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 5 Jun 2029 17:42:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Intermittent throughput collapse — firewall sessions & DNS cache storms (BrightRoot)

BrightRoot team,

Thank you for the quick signal and patience while we investigated. Summary of status:

- What happened: a combination of aggressive short-lived connections from a third-party partner and an internal DNS resolver config regression led to extremely low effective TTLs and a flood of queries; this increased connection churn which in turn exhausted the firewall session table on edge-gw-3. That combination produced elevated latency and 5xxs for your checkout traffic.
- What we did: increased session table, tuned SYN backlogs, failed over DNS, applied temporary SYN-rate limits, and provisioned Dedicated burst capacity in us-east while we harden rules.
- Outcome: traffic normalized by 2029-06-03 12:10 PDT. No further degradation reported; we will keep monitoring for 72 hours.

Next steps:
1) We'll run a joint review with BrightRoot next week (propose Thu 2029-06-13 10:00 PDT) — please let us know availability.
2) We'll deliver a full postmortem draft and remedial action plan by EOD 2029-06-07 (attached draft: postmortem-draft.md).
3) We will propose peering policy changes and a resolver config guardrail to prevent regression.

Marcus Lin
VP, Platform
Redwood Inference

title: Priority eviction policy & queue weighting for Dedicated pools
preview:
From: Sasha Ivanov <sasha@vectorlabs.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@vectorlabs.com
Date: Mon, 18 Sep 2028 16:12:00 -0400
Subject: Dedicated pool: eviction priority / queue weighting questions

Hey Alex,

Quick follow up from our internal architecture review — we want to lock down how Redwood will handle ephemeral/headroom capacity and preemptible GPUs for the Dedicated pool in the VectorLabs deal. Two specific asks:

1) Eviction ordering and notification: what gets evicted first (ephemeral jobs, low-priority background requests, or live inference flows)? Do we provide any preemption notice callbacks so we can gracefully drain sessions?

2) Queue weighting / priority lanes: we have three traffic classes (interactive low-latency, batch/async pipeline, and background retraining). We need a concrete priority matrix and expected SLOs during a transient surge (expected peak ~220k tokens/sec across models, steady ~45k).

We’re looking at a committed baseline of 40 A100-equivalent GPUs with shoulder capacity for burst. We need to understand: allowed percent of preemptible capacity, typical preemption windows, and the commercial crediting model tied to preemption or throttling.

Can you share a document that shows the eviction priority matrix, the autoscale cooldowns/step sizes, and sample SLA language that covers credits during preemptions? Procurement copied on this thread is keen to review.

Thanks — Sasha
Sasha Ivanov
Head of Platform Engineering, VectorLabs

---
Attachment: system-traffic-estimates.csv (referenced)

From: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
To: Sasha Ivanov <sasha@vectorlabs.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, procurement@vectorlabs.com
Date: Mon, 18 Sep 2028 18:04:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Dedicated pool: eviction priority / queue weighting questions

Thanks Sasha — good set of questions. I put together an initial priority/eviction outline and a simple queue-weighting approach; attaching a short matrix (priority_matrix.xlsx) and a one-page summary below. This is the starting point — Karthik and Marcus, please weigh in on technical and contract implications.

Summary (high level):
- Lanes: Reserved (commit), Shoulder (pay-as-you-burst up to X), Ephemeral (preemptible spot-like pool).
- Eviction ordering (highest -> lowest priority to keep): Reserved traffic -> Shoulder high-priority -> Shoulder low-priority -> Ephemeral.
- Preemption notice: 30s lightweight signal + 5m graceful-drain webhook support for interactive sessions (we can extend to 15m for long-running jobs by contract).
- Queue weighting: use exponential decay weights with 3 pools mapped to the traffic classes; interactive gets 60% of scoring weight, batch 30%, background 10% during contention. We’ll provide runtime knobs per-route.
- Autoscale defaults: scale step = 10% of pool size, cooldown = 90s, max burst = 50% of committed baseline (subject to capacity).

Next actions: Karthik to confirm preemption behavior and cache handling; Marcus to propose commercial limits (allowed preemptible % and crediting). I’ll prepare a draft SLA and include the eviction matrix.

Regards,
Alex Martinez
Account Lead, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference

---
Attachment: priority_matrix.xlsx (stub)

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>, Sasha Ivanov <sasha@vectorlabs.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 19 Sep 2028 09:38:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Dedicated pool: eviction priority / queue weighting questions

High-level technical follow up from infra side. A few concrete constraints and recommended configs to make the behavior predictable and safe:

1) Preemptible pool behavior:
- Default preemptible cap: 20% of total committed GPUs. We can increase to 30% for a negotiated fee.
- Eviction order: tasks on preemptible GPUs are first to be reclaimed. Within preemptible, idle/queued jobs are reclaimed before active inferences with active SSE (session) tokens.
- Preemption notice: we will emit a short GRPC/webhook 30s signal and an optional 5-minute drain window for sessions. For long-running jobs (large-batch training or very long-gen), we recommend 15-minute notice as part of the shoulder contract.

2) Autoscaling policy (recommended defaults):
- Metric: 95th-percentile request-queue-time + GPU util delta triggers.
- Scale up: add min(8 GPUs, 10% of pool) every 45s if sustained over 2 datapoints (2 x 45s).
- Scale down: remove 5% of pool after 10 minutes under low-util. Use staggered removal to avoid cache-thrashing.
- Cooldown window: 5 minutes after a scale event before reversing.

3) Cache and model fallback:
- When preemption or eviction occurs we will attempt to persist KV cache to a short-lived LRU store for 10 minutes to reduce cold-start token cost. If cold-start occurs, route to lighter quantized variant (q4_8b) automatically as fallback.

4) Observability:
- We will surface preemption events to your telemetry stream and expose a preemption_count metric and average_drain_time.

Note on cost & risk: increasing preemptible cap reduces committed cost but increases cold-start probability for interactive sessions. Recommend baseline 40 GPUs, shoulder up to +20 (burst to 60) and preemptible 15-20% for initial contract.

-Karthik
Principal Infra Eng, Redwood Inference

---
Quoted text from earlier:
> From: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
> Summary (high level):
> - Lanes: Reserved (commit), Shoulder (pay-as-you-burst up to X), Ephemeral (preemptible spot-like pool).

From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Sasha Ivanov <sasha@vectorlabs.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, procurement@vectorlabs.com
Date: Tue, 19 Sep 2028 11:07:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Dedicated pool: eviction priority / queue weighting questions

Thanks — commercial notes and constraints:

- Credit model: propose SLA credit bands tied to sustained preemption-caused error rate on the interactive route. Example: >1% user-visible error over a 1h window => 5% monthly credit; >3% => 15% credit.
- Allowed preemptible percentage: default 20% in the SOW. If VectorLabs wants 30% we need a higher burst premium and reduced SLA tiers.
- Shoulder billing: pay-as-you-burst will be invoiced at 1.6x hosted per-GPU-minute for shoulder minutes beyond baseline. We can cap monthly shoulder spend if needed.
- Contract language: we should call out a max preemption notice of 5 minutes for interactive routes, and a guaranteed warm-pool of 5 GPUs for Reserved traffic to reduce cold starts.

I’ll draft the commercial bullets and fold into the proposed-sla.pdf. Monica, please check that our suggested credit bands are within standard precedence.

~Marcus
Head of Sales Engineering, Redwood Inference

- VectorLabs is comfortable with a 15% preemptible cap to start if we get a 5-minute preemption notice for interactive flows and a guaranteed warm-pool of at least 4 GPUs for Reserved traffic.

Subject: Re: Dedicated pool: eviction priority / queue weighting questions

Thanks Sasha — good set of questions. I put together an initial priority/eviction outline and a simple queue-weighting approach; attaching a short matrix (priority_matrix.xlsx) and a one-page summary below. This is the starting point — Karthik and Marcus, please weigh in on technical and contract implications.

Summary (high level):
- Lanes: Reserved (commit), Shoulder (pay-as-you-burst up to X), Ephemeral (preemptible spot-like pool).
- Eviction ordering (highest -> lowest priority to keep): Reserved traffic -> Shoulder high-priority -> Shoulder low-priority -> Ephemeral.
- Preemption notice: 30s lightweight signal + 5m graceful-drain webhook support for interactive sessions (we can extend to 15m for long-running jobs by contract).
- Queue weighting: use exponential decay weights with 3 pools mapped to the traffic classes; interactive gets 60% of scoring weight, batch 30%, background 10% during contention. We’ll provide runtime knobs per-route.
- Autoscale defaults: scale step = 10% of pool size, cooldown = 90s, max burst = 50% of committed baseline (subject to capacity).

Next actions: Karthik to confirm preemption behavior and cache handling; Marcus to propose commercial limits (allowed preemptible % and crediting). I’ll prepare a draft SLA and include the eviction matrix.

Regards,
Alex Martinez
Account Lead, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>, Sasha Ivanov <sasha@vectorlabs.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 19 Sep 2028 09:38:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Dedicated pool: eviction priority / queue weighting questions

High-level technical follow up from infra side. A few concrete constraints and recommended configs to make the behavior predictable and safe:

1) Preemptible pool behavior:
- Default preemptible cap: 20% of total committed GPUs. We can increase to 30% for a negotiated fee.
- Eviction order: tasks on preemptible GPUs are first to be reclaimed. Within preemptible, idle/queued jobs are reclaimed before active inferences with active SSE (session) tokens.
- Preemption notice: we will emit a short GRPC/webhook 30s signal and an optional 5-minute drain window for sessions. For long-running jobs (large-batch training or very long-gen), we recommend 15-minute notice as part of the shoulder contract.

2) Autoscaling policy (recommended defaults):
- Metric: 95th-percentile request-queue-time + GPU util delta triggers.
- Scale up: add min(8 GPUs, 10% of pool) every 45s if sustained over 2 datapoints (2 x 45s).
- Scale down: remove 5% of pool after 10 minutes under low-util. Use staggered removal to avoid cache-thrashing.
- Cooldown window: 5 minutes after a scale event before reversing.

3) Cache and model fallback:
- When preemption or eviction occurs we will attempt to persist KV cache to a short-lived LRU store for 10 minutes to reduce cold-start token cost. If cold-start occurs, route to lighter quantized variant (q4_8b) automatically as fallback.

4) Observability:
- We will surface preemption events to your telemetry stream and expose a preemption_count metric and average_drain_time.

Note on cost & risk: increasing preemptible cap reduces committed cost but increases cold-start probability for interactive sessions. Recommend baseline 40 GPUs, shoulder up to +20 (burst to 60) and preemptible 15-20% for initial contract.

-Karthik
Principal Infra Eng, Redwood Inference

title: Finexa P1: sudden 429s and latency spike — suspected token-bucket / burst policy issue
preview:
From: Sasha Green <sasha.green@finexa.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-06-14 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: High 429 rate + latency spike impacting Finexa payment flows

Alex / Laura —

We started seeing a sharp uptick in 429s and end-to-end latency across our payment validation flows at ~10:05am PT. Error rate jumped from ~0.1% to ~8% and tail latency moved from 120ms -> 1.2s. Customers are seeing failed authorizations.

Attached is a screenshot of the dashboard and a sample request/response showing 429: rate_limit and the token-bucket header showing \"dropped=1\". This looks like throttling on your side — can you confirm whether any quota or burst policy changed?

This is P1 for us; we need an update within 30 minutes.

Thanks,
Sasha Green
Sr. Platform Ops, Finexa
(sasha.green@finexa.com)

Attachment: screenshot_429s.png


From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Sasha Green <sasha.green@finexa.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-06-14 10:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: High 429 rate + latency spike impacting Finexa payment flows

Sasha — thanks for the ping and the screenshot. We see the same spike on our side (matching traces). I’m looping in Marcus to triage the policy/config side and Rachel/Laura to coordinate with your ops and procurement if we need to provide immediate relief (temporary quota bump / credits).

Immediate plan:
- Marcus: can you confirm whether a policy rollout or a default quota change hit Finexa’s org id around 10:00–10:10?
- If this is a burst-policy/token-bucket misconfiguration, propose an emergency rollback to the previous policy for Finexa and disable strict burst enforcement for 30 mins while we stabilize.
- Rachel/Laura: please prepare a customer-facing note and let Sasha know if we need to open a credits/commit exception.

I’ll keep Sasha updated every 15 minutes until we’re stable.

— Alex
Alex Martinez
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
alex.martinez@redwood.ai


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-06-14 10:35:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: High 429 rate + latency spike impacting Finexa payment flows

On Fri, Jun 14, 2028 at 10:22 AM Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Marcus: can you confirm whether a policy rollout or a default quota change hit Finexa’s org id around 10:00–10:10?

I pulled the policy audit and traffic traces. Root cause summary: during the 09:55 global quota-policy push we accidentally applied a new token-bucket profile that set \"burst_capacity\"=0 for a cohort which includes Finexa (policy selector mismatch). The token-bucket algorithm then rejected burst tokens instead of allowing short spikes, which manifests as 429s and raised latency due to retries.

Evidence: SUP-9876 shows the rollout diff and the applied selector. I’m attaching a perf snapshot and a short log snippet.

Mitigation options (fastest first):
1) Emergency rollback of the policy version for Finexa org id (hotfix): expected recovery within ~3–8 minutes after rollback.
2) If rollback risks other customers, temporarily set burst_capacity=2000 for Finexa via an override (takes ~2–5 minutes to apply).
3) Longer term: add a rollout guard that prevents zero-burst policies from targeting non-test orgs.

I recommend immediate rollback. If I get approval I’ll push the rollback and monitor.

— Marcus
Marcus Lin
Senior Software Engineer, Serving Runtime
marcus.lin@redwood.ai

Attachments: perf_snapshot_20280614.json
Related: https://jira.redwood.ai/browse/SUP-9876

Subject: Re: High 429 rate + latency spike impacting Finexa payment flows

Sasha — update: we performed the emergency rollback at 15:12 PT and removed the strict burst enforcement for Finexa for a 45-minute window. Post-rollback metrics: 429 rate returned to baseline (~0.15%) within ~5 minutes; p95 latency back to ~130ms. We’ve attached the policy_rollback.log and a short note describing the changes.

Next steps we’ll own:
- Permanent guard in the rollout service to prevent zero-burst profiles from matching production orgs.
- Postmortem + RCA published in SUP-9876 by EOD.
- Rachel will follow up on the credit/contract ask.

Appreciate the patience and sorry for the disruption — we’ll keep you posted and will provide the postmortem.

Best,
Alex Martinez
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

From: Sasha Green <sasha.green@finexa.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-06-14 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: High 429 rate + latency spike impacting Finexa payment flows

Alex / Laura —

We started seeing a sharp uptick in 429s and end-to-end latency across our payment validation flows at ~10:05am PT. Error rate jumped from ~0.1% to ~8% and tail latency moved from 120ms -> 1.2s. Customers are seeing failed authorizations.

Attached is a screenshot of the dashboard and a sample request/response showing 429: rate_limit and the token-bucket header showing \"dropped=1\". This looks like throttling on your side — can you confirm whether any quota or burst policy changed?

This is P1 for us; we need an update within 30 minutes.

Thanks,
Sasha Green
Sr. Platform Ops, Finexa
(sasha.green@finexa.com)

Attachment: screenshot_429s.png


From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Sasha Green <sasha.green@finexa.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-06-14 10:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: High 429 rate + latency spike impacting Finexa payment flows

Sasha — thanks for the ping and the screenshot. We see the same spike on our side (matching traces). I’m looping in Marcus to triage the policy/config side and Rachel/Laura to coordinate with your ops and procurement if we need to provide immediate relief (temporary quota bump / credits).

Immediate plan:
- Marcus: can you confirm whether a policy rollout or a default quota change hit Finexa’s org id around 10:00–10:10?
- If this is a burst-policy/token-bucket misconfiguration, propose an emergency rollback to the previous policy for Finexa and disable strict burst enforcement for 30 mins while we stabilize.
- Rachel/Laura: please prepare a customer-facing note and let Sasha know if we need to open a credits/commit exception.

I’ll keep Sasha updated every 15 minutes until we’re stable.

— Alex
Alex Martinez
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
alex.martinez@redwood.ai


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-06-14 10:35:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: High 429 rate + latency spike impacting Finexa payment flows

On Fri, Jun 14, 2028 at 10:22 AM Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Marcus: can you confirm whether a policy rollout or a default quota change hit Finexa’s org id around 10:00–10:10?

I pulled the policy audit and traffic traces. Root cause summary: during the 09:55 global quota-policy push we accidentally applied a new token-bucket profile that set \"burst_capacity\"=0 for a cohort which includes Finexa (policy selector mismatch). The token-bucket algorithm then rejected burst tokens instead of allowing short spikes, which manifests as 429s and raised latency due to retries.

Evidence: SUP-9876 shows the rollout diff and the applied selector. I’m attaching a perf snapshot and a short log snippet.

Mitigation options (fastest first):
1) Emergency rollback of the policy version for Finexa org id (hotfix): expected recovery within ~3–8 minutes after rollback.
2) If rollback risks other customers, temporarily set burst_capacity=2000 for Finexa via an override (takes ~2–5 minutes to apply).
3) Longer term: add a rollout guard that prevents zero-burst policies from targeting non-test orgs.

I recommend immediate rollback. If I get approval I’ll push the rollback and monitor.

— Marcus
Marcus Lin
Senior Software Engineer, Serving Runtime
marcus.lin@redwood.ai

Attachments: perf_snapshot_20280614.json
Related: https://jira.redwood.ai/browse/SUP-9876

Team — attaching the recommended Q3 leadership slate for your review (Q3_Leadership_Slate_v2.xlsx) and the proposed comp bands document (Compensation_Bands_Q3_v1.pdf). Highlights up front:
- Slate includes two VP-level hires (Platform Eng, Ops) and one Director->VP internal uplift for Product.
- Net incremental HC: +2.5 FTE (one replacement counted at 0.5) — fits within budget envelope with the revised ramp assumptions.
- Compensation bands updated to reflect mid‑year market movement; small adjustments in VP target 50th/75th lines.

Asks: Vivek — can you confirm whether you are OK with CEO-level signoff (I can route the final memo to you for signature), or do you want this escalated to the board liaisons?

Aditya — please confirm runway math (tab 'BudgetImpact' in the spreadsheet) matches your latest forecast; I flagged one conservative hiring timing assumption (VP Ops start set to 9/1).
Marcus — quick note: the Platform VP is a backfill for an early departure; we recommend hiring at the mid-to-upper quartile to reduce time-to-accept. Your thoughts on market urgency?

Next steps: if I get signoff today I will prepare the CEO memo and a one-page summary for the board packet. I also copied Cassidy from Talent Partners — she's primed to accelerate candidate outreach once bands approved.

Attachments: Q3_Leadership_Slate_v2.xlsx (spreadsheet), Compensation_Bands_Q3_v1.pdf (pdf)

title: SOC2 Type II requested — NDA gating and bridge letter process
preview:
From: Simon Trent <simon@finledger.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Liz Howard <liz.howard@finledger.com>
Date: Tue, 30 May 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Request for SOC 2 Type II report and evidence access

Hi Ava,

We are starting our security review for a platform integration and would like to request your SOC 2 Type II report for the last audit period. Two quick questions to scope this:

- Will the report be shared under an NDA? Our procurement team requires countersigned NDAs on file before viewing any privileged reports.
- If you cannot share the full report, do you have a bridge/attestation letter that covers the same period (or a note about the audit window)?

Timeline: we are aiming to complete vendor reviews by June 15, so would appreciate next steps this week if possible.

Thanks — happy to hop on a short call if that's easier.

Simon Trent
Security Lead, FinLedger
Simon.Trent@finledger.com
+1 (415) 555-0119

Attachment: FinLedger-Vendor-Security-Checklist.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet)


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Simon Trent <simon@finledger.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 30 May 2028 10:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request for SOC 2 Type II report and evidence access

Hi Simon — thanks for the note and the checklist. Looping in Marcus (Security Ops) and Grace (Customer Success) to help land this.

Short answers up front:
- Yes — our SOC 2 Type II report is treated as privileged and we require a countersigned NDA before providing access to the full PDF and supporting artifacts.
- We can provide a bridge letter that confirms the audit period and the scope of controls while the NDA is being processed.

Proposed plan:
1) I am attaching our standard NDA template (Redwood_NDA_Template_v3.docx). If your legal team prefers to send a countersigned version, please do so and we will countersign on our side.
2) We'll provide a draft bridge letter (Redwood_SOC2_Bridge_Letter_Draft.pdf) within 24 hours that you can use for your internal reviewers.
3) Once we have a countersigned NDA, we'll deliver a secure, time-limited link to the report (PDF) and a sealed evidence bundle (logs and control mappings) via our secure share.

Action items for FinLedger:
- Please return a countersigned NDA to ava.chen@redwood.ai (or sign electronically via DocuSign link below).
- Confirm the audit window you need (e.g., last 12 months or specific period).

DocuSign link (optional): https://app.redwood.ai/docusign/BRIDGE-DEAL-1289

We typically expect 1–2 business days to provision the secure share after NDA is countersigned. If you need an expedited process, let us know and Marcus will prioritize.

Best,
Ava Chen
Head of Sales Engineering — Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 (650) 555-0247

Attachments: Redwood_NDA_Template_v3.docx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document), Redwood_SOC2_Bridge_Letter_Draft.pdf (application/pdf)

Subject: Re: Request for SOC 2 Type II report and evidence access

Hi Ava — thanks for the quick response.

I've attached a countersigned NDA (FinLedger_Countersigned_NDA.pdf) — please countersign on your side and confirm. We reviewed the bridge letter draft and it looks fine for our procurement team.

Audit window: we only need the most recent Type II report covering 2027-01-01 through 2027-12-31.

If you can provide the secure link after countersign and a short readme outlining which files are included in the evidence bundle (control mappings, Type II report, SOC2 exception logs), that will help our reviewers.

Liz Howard
Vendor Risk Manager, FinLedger
liz.howard@finledger.com

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Liz Howard <liz.howard@finledger.com>, Simon Trent <simon@finledger.com>

Date: Wed, 31 May 2028 15:46:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request for SOC 2 Type II report and evidence access

Liz / Simon — received, thanks. Ava and I will countersign the NDA today.

Next steps (confirmed):
- Redwood will countersign the NDA and return an executed PDF by end of day (attached to this thread).
- We will issue a time-limited, watermarked link to the SOC 2 Type II PDF covering 2027-01-01 to 2027-12-31 and the evidence bundle (control mappings, exception summaries, and sampling artifacts). Expect the secure link within 24 hours of NDA countersign.

Notes for FinLedger reviewers: the evidence bundle contains redacted operational details (IP addresses, internal session identifiers) by default — if your security team needs less redaction for an internal audit, please raise that as a separate request and we'll provide a scoped SSR (secured on-premise review) plan.

title: Post-signature orchestration & MAP: VantageRx rollout, canary, migration, training
preview:
From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
To: Emily Park <emily.park@vantagerx.com>, procurement@vantagerx.com
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 10 May 2028 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Post-signature orchestration & MAP: VantageRx rollout, canary, migration, training

Hi Emily / Kevin / team,

First—congratulations again on signing. Attached is the initial Mutual Action Plan (MAP) workbook and a short evaluation plan that we put together based on our discussions. High-level ask: can you confirm a preferred kickoff week and an initial canary window? We propose a 2-week canary starting the week of May 22, with a staged migration thereafter.

Key items in the MAP (attached):
- Phase 0 (pre-kick): account provisioning, KMS onboarding, SOC2 evidence exchange — target complete by May 18
- Phase 1 (canary): 2-week canary (limited routes, 10% traffic) — proposed May 22–June 4
- Phase 2 (ramp): progressive traffic ramp and model tuning — targeted Jun 5–Jun 18
- Phase 3 (training & handoff): end-user training, ops runbook, SLA checkpoints — Jun 19–Jun 30

Immediate asks:
1) Confirm kickoff date (we penciled May 17 for a short alignment call).
2) Share procurement PO or invoicing contact for billing cadence.
3) Confirm whether you want the canary limited by route (recommended) or by region.

Attachments included: VantageRx_MAP_20280510.xlsx (MAP), EvaluationPlan_VRX_20280510.pdf (eval prompt set + success criteria), SOC2_Redwood_2027.pdf (requested security evidence).

We'll assign owners on our side—Vanessa will lead operations (canary gates, rollout), Marcus will lead the migration automation and infra validation. I'll coordinate the executive cadence and risk decisions.

Thanks,
Rishi Malhotra
Head of Sales Engineering
Redwood Inference

[Attachment: VantageRx_MAP_20280510.xlsx (spreadsheet)]
[Attachment: EvaluationPlan_VRX_20280510.pdf (pdf)]
[Attachment: SOC2_Redwood_2027.pdf (pdf)]

To: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@vantagerx.com
Date: Fri, 11 May 2028 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-signature orchestration & MAP: VantageRx rollout, canary, migration, training

Hi Rishi—thanks and congrats all around.

We're aligned on the proposed phases. Quick responses:
- Kickoff: May 17 works for a 45-minute alignment call (please send invite).
- Canary: prefer route-limited canary (we have a high-volume claims route we want excluded during canary).
- Security evidence: SOC2 is fine, but procurement will need a slightly longer retention policy summary and an answer on data residency (we're US-only).

Procurement note: I've looped Kevin Li (cc'd) and our procurement alias. He'll confirm PO timing and PO number.

Also: for the EvaluationPlan, can you confirm whether the eval harness will run against production-sim traffic or a mirrored stream? We prefer mirrored if possible.

Looking forward—EM

Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 12 May 2028 08:21:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-signature orchestration & MAP: VantageRx rollout, canary, migration, training

Emily—thanks. A few clarifications and concrete owners below.

Evaluation harness: we recommend mirrored traffic for the initial canary to avoid impacting live users; Redwood's runtime supports stream mirroring with anonymization hooks. The EvaluationPlan (attached) includes the prompt set, pass/fail thresholds (latency p95 < 450ms, error rate < 0.5%), and manual QA checkpoints for subjective quality.

Owners (Redwood):
- Vanessa Ortiz: Canary gates, rollback/runbook owner (vanessa@redwood.com)
- Marcus Lin: Infra validation, VPC/KMS onboarding, automation scripts (marcus@redwood.com)
- Rishi Malhotra: Exec updates, change approvals, escalation point (rishi@redwood.com)

Proposed week-by-week MAP milestones (excerpt from workbook):
- May 17: Kickoff (alignment call) — confirm routes in scope, PO status, DocuSign MSA signature
- May 18–21: Provisioning & keys — VPC peering, KMS key import, whitelist IPs
- May 22–Jun 4: Canary (mirrored traffic on non-claims routes) — daily health reports, canary gates on token cost and latency
- Jun 5–Jun 18: Progressive ramp to 60% traffic — model tuning windows and regression checks
- Jun 19–Jun 30: Training sessions (ops + developer) and SLA handover; final acceptance sign-off

We're adding one additional deliverable to the MAP: a short runbook named "VRX-Canary-Gates.md" that enumerates automatic rollback triggers. That will be posted to the shared drive after kickoff.

I've attached the MAP workbook and EvaluationPlan again for easy reference.

Thanks—I'll schedule the May 17 kickoff for 9:30am PT. Invite to follow.

Rishi

[Attachment: VantageRx_MAP_20280510.xlsx (spreadsheet)]
[Attachment: EvaluationPlan_VRX_20280510.pdf (pdf)]

From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa@redwood.com>
To: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Emily Park <emily.park@vantagerx.com>
Date: Mon, 13 May 2028 11:46:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-signature orchestration & MAP: VantageRx rollout, canary, migration, training

Looping in a few operational checklist items and quick questions so procurement and platform teams have visibility.

Operational checklist (short):
- Confirm IP allowlist and CIDR blocks for peering by May 18
- KMS key exchange and asymmetric key wrap test (Marcus to run)
- Canary traffic filter: provide exact route patterns to include/exclude (Emily to confirm by May 16)
- Monitoring: we'll provide Grafana dashboard link + runbook alerts (thresholds in MAP)
- Backout plan: immediate fallback to smaller model family + rate-limit reduction (documented in VRX-Canary-Gates.md)

Questions for VantageRx:
1) Data retention policy for logs: do you require logs to be retained >90 days? If so we'll create a scoped ingest policy and map that to your retention SLA.
2) Will you require a private endpoint (VPC) only or are there any public-facing routes?

On quoting the evaluation point—mirrored traffic is the safest path. For the canary let's plan 1 week of mirror + 1 week of low-percentage live routing, if that aligns with Emily's request.

Thanks—Vanessa

Vanessa Ortiz
Principal Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference

title: Production service suppression at Lumenix — suspected quota/burst policy issue
preview:
From: Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Hi Ava,

We're seeing a sharp drop in our throughput to Redwood (from ~800 rps to under 60 rps) starting ~08:45 PT this morning. Error rate spiked with 429s and end-to-end latency jumped from ~120ms to >1.2s. This impacted two of our customer-facing services.

Attached is a short traffic dump and a couple of example request IDs. Can your team confirm if this is on Redwood's side (rate limiting / quota / burst) or if we should look at our client-side retries? It's blocking our morning release.

Request IDs: rq_0f3b2a, rq_0f3b2b
Attachments: lumenix_traffic_snippet_20280314.csv (CSV excerpt, 2MB)

We need a short call if this is something you can resolve quickly — available 09:30 or 10:00 PT.

Thanks,
Samir
Samir Khan
Head of Platform, Lumenix
samir@lumenix.io


From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 09:35:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Folks —

Forwarding an inbound from Lumenix. Impacting production; customer reports big spike in 429s + latency. Samir attached a short traffic dump. Can infra/SRE quickly assess whether this looks like a rate-limit/quota/burst policy enforcement or something else?

Samir — I'm pulling Karthik and Monica into the thread for immediate investigation. Marcus and Marissa are looped for visibility on escalation/credits if needed.

I can join a 09:30 PT call if that helps.

Thanks,
Ava

----Forwarded message----


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 10:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Ava / team —

Quick triage summary after looking at Samir's dump and our control plane logs:
- Observed enforcement events from the rate-safety valve at 08:46 PT targeted at Lumenix's customer-id.
- The enforcement counter shows consecutive burst-window hits; quota window alignment appears wrong (30s burst window being interpreted as 300ms due to an off-by-factor config on the routing layer for this tenant).
- Result: continuous short-term throttling (many tokens denied) and backoff cascades on the client side leading to large perceived latency.

I've attached a config snapshot from the control plane (config_snapshot_20280314.json). Highlights:
- policy_id: psb-tenant-window-42
- burst.window_ms: 300 (expected 30000)
- enforcement_mode: permissive -> erroneously flipped to strict during a config push at 02:12 PT (suspect rollback did not complete)

Immediate recommended mitigations (short term):
1) Revert the burst.window_ms to 30000 for the tenant and reload the policy (monica).
2) Temporarily raise the tenant soft quota by 2x and allow increased burst tokens to drain backlog.
3) Enable automatic fallback routing to dedicated nodes for Lumenix for the next 4 hours (reduces policy surface).

I'll stay on the call. If you want I can run the revert and monitor.

-Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Senior Infra Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 10:25:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

On Wed, Mar 14, 2028 at 10:02 AM Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com> wrote:
> Immediate recommended mitigations (short term):
> 1) Revert the burst.window_ms to 30000 for the tenant and reload the policy (monica).
> 2) Temporarily raise the tenant soft quota by 2x and allow increased burst tokens to drain backlog.
> 3) Enable automatic fallback routing to dedicated nodes for Lumenix for the next 4 hours (reduces policy surface).

I've executed items 1 and 2 at 10:18 PT and am monitoring.
Actions taken:
- Reverted policy psb-tenant-window-42 -> burst.window_ms set to 30000 and reloaded. (config change id: cfg-20280314-019)
- Raised soft quota for tenant lumenix-cust-12 by 2x until 14:30 PT.
- Opened INF ticket: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/INF-7123 (SRE triage + postmortem)

Early results: enforcement events dropped significantly; 429s are down to baseline in our telemetry. I'm leaving elevated quotas in place until 14:30 PT and will coordinate rollback once telemetry stabilizes.

I'll keep Samir posted in this thread.

-Monica
Monica Patel
SRE, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Cc: Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 11:10:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Team —

Thanks for the fast action. Monica/Karthik, good notes on the root cause (config drift -> off-by-factor window). From a commercial standpoint Lumenix got significant customer impact this morning.

Please prepare a short customer-facing summary (what happened, what we changed, what we will do to prevent recurrence) and let's propose a remediation package: 10% credit for the incident window + an offer for a 30-day free trial of Dedicated capacity (to avoid tenant surface policy issues). Ava, can you coordinate the wording with Samir and confirm if they want the Trial?

I'll escalate to Exec Ops for approval of the credit and note the incident in tomorrow's leadership standup. INF-7123 should capture the postmortem and follow-up action items.

-Marissa
Marissa Cole
VP, Sales Operations, Redwood Inference


From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 16 Mar 2028 08:43:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Hi Samir —

Thanks again for reporting this quickly and for the sample traces. Monica reverted the burst window and we temporarily increased quota while traffic drained; telemetry shows error-rate and latency back to baseline. We've attached a short mutual action plan that includes the remediation steps, timeline, and proposed Dedicated trial details.

Attachments: mutual_action_plan_lumenix.docx

Proposed next steps:
- 30-min wrap-up call Monday 10:00 PT to review the INF-7123 postmortem and agree on Dedicated pilot dates.
- We'll provision a 14-day Dedicated trial environment and feed you a migration checklist.
- Marissa will propose the credit in a separate note once Exec Ops confirms.

Please let me know if Monday 10:00 works or suggest another slot.

Best,
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.com
Phone: +1 (415) 555-0128

Subject: Intro: connector sourcing for vector storage

Hi Avery / team,

Thanks for taking a quick look — we had a brief internal sync at Pinecone about your connector requirements sheet and wanted to kick off a lightweight intake. Goal: align on the minimal technical checklist and the preferred commercial path (co-sell vs. marketplace listing vs. integration referral).

Quick context: we're focusing on a hardened connector + cert workflow that covers:
- index lifecycle (create/delete/replicate)
- auth patterns (service tokens, ephemeral creds)
- fallthrough to S3/pgvector for cold tiers
- observability hooks (request-level tracing, error codes)

Would love a 45m intake next week with engineering + partner ops. I attached a short worksheet (connector_requirements_v1.xlsx) showing the things we commonly need.

Proposed times: Tue 3/14 9–10am PT or Wed 3/15 11–11:45am PT.

Cheers,
Sam Reed
Partner Integrations, Pinecone
attachments: connector_requirements_v1.xlsx (xlsx)

title: Post-call: NimbleCart throughput & streaming snapshot — next steps
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@nimblecart.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 21 Feb 2028 15:12:00 -0800
Subject: Post-call recap + traffic/profile asks (throughput & streaming)

Thanks for taking the time today — quick recap from our discovery so we can size and propose the right pilot. Please see bullets and the specific asks at the bottom.

What we heard on the call:
- Primary product: real-time order recommendations surfaced during checkout.
- 60% of requests are streaming responses (progressive recommendations) vs 40% full-response.
- Target e2e SLO: 95th percentile start-of-stream < 300ms, full-response 95th < 700ms for median payloads.
- Typical request: 12 tokens prompt, ~70 tokens average response, but peak flows can have 200+ tokens/resp.
- Traffic pattern: steady baseline during day with short bursts (15–45s) during promotions. Peak windows observed ~10x steady QPS.

Key unknowns we need to size correctly (please share if available):
1) 48–72h sample of request logs (timestamp, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, streaming flag) OR a CSV export (see example attached).
2) Peak QPS and steady-state QPS by hour/day (if you have 95th/99th percentiles for windows that helps).
3) Typical concurrency per user/session, and throttling behavior in-app (max concurrent open streams).
4) Any strict bandwidth/region constraints (we heard US-only but confirm).

Our initial hypotheses (to validate):
- Steady-state: ~150–300 QPS (depending on region split).
- Burst peak: ~1,200–2,000 QPS for 30–90s during promotions.
- Avg tokens/sec baseline: nominal; token-based batching will meaningfully change cost at peak.

Next steps / asks:
- Emily: share the 48–72h CSV sample and any recent peak windows (attach to HubSpot / Drive).
- Jordan + Marcus: we’ll run a quick sizing sweep once we have the sample and return a short proposal (hosted vs dedicated recommendation with rough cost & attendees for pilot).
- Goal: draft a pilot plan and SLA note by Thu (48 hours) so NimbleCart can review with infra/procurement.

Attachments: sample CSV template attached (nimble_example_traffic.csv).

Thanks — Ava
Ava Chen
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.com

---
(Attachment: nimble_example_traffic.csv — CSV stub, expected columns: timestamp, request_id, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, streaming_bool, region)

From: Emily Zhang <emily@nimblecart.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: NimbleCart Infra <infra@nimblecart.com>
Date: Tue, 21 Feb 2028 17:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + traffic/profile asks (throughput & streaming)

Thanks Ava — great recap. Attaching a 72h export and a short SLA doc for context (nimble_sla_requirements.pdf). High level answers inline: 

- Steady-state QPS (US region): ~220 QPS (weekday daytime).
- Observed promotional peaks: 1,800–2,100 QPS for ~30–60s windows; happens ~3–5x/day during promotions.
- Token distribution: median response ~68 tokens, 75th ~120 tokens, occasional long tails to 350 tokens. Prompt tokens mostly 8–20.
- Streaming: ~60% of calls are streaming; client opens up to 6 concurrent streams per user during heavy checkout sessions.
- Regional constraint: US primary, EU negligible for now.

We attached nimble_example_traffic.csv (48h sample: includes a 45s peak window). Let us know if the columns/format don’t work. We also want an initial cost ballpark and whether you think Hosted API will meet the SLOs or if we should plan Dedicated.

Emily Zhang
Head of Infra, Product
NimbleCart
emily@nimblecart.com

(Attachment: nimble_example_traffic.csv — 48h sample, includes a 45s peak window at 2028-02-19 14:22:xx UTC)

From: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@nimblecart.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 22 Feb 2028 08:09:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + traffic/profile asks (throughput & streaming)

Appreciate the data, Emily — dug into the CSV quickly and here are initial sizing notes (high-level, conservative):

Observations from sample:
- Steady: ~210 QPS, median response ~68 tokens, streaming fraction ~58%.
- Peak window (45s): sustained ~1,950 QPS mapped to many short-lived sessions.

Initial sizing guidance (to budget/propose pilot):
- Hosted (fast time-to-value): can likely meet the SLOs for steady-state with appropriate batching and streaming optimizations. For peaks, Hosted with auto-burst may work but risk of throttling depending on other tenants — recommend a committed Dedicated lane for predictable peak handling.
- Suggested pilot footprint: 2 x dedicated GPU nodes for steady-state (handles ~250 QPS comfortably with batching + cache). Add burst pool sizing: 6–8 nodes or equivalent autoscale to handle 1,800–2,000 QPS with target 95th start-of-stream < 300ms. Exact node type TBD based on quantization profile and kernel mix.
- Token throughput: at 70 tokens/resp, peak token/sec at 2,000 QPS ≈ 140k tokens/sec — this drives batching and memory/KV cache needs.
- Concurrency: client opens up to 6 streams/user — we need to model concurrent KV cache pressure and connection limits.

Short asks from our side to finalize numbers:
1) Can you confirm any hard SLA on cost vs latency for promotions? (procurement input).
2) Provide a 1-hour high-resolution log around a representative peak (preferably raw request-level timestamps) so we can run a burst simulation.

Jordan will draft a 2-page pilot proposal (hosted vs dedicated tradeoff, estimated cost range, success criteria) and share by EOD. Ava / Marcus will pick the proposal owners.

-Jordan
Jordan Blake
Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference

title: Re: production latency & error surge — logs / audit handling questions
preview:
From: Jordan Clark <jordan.clark@apexpay.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>; ops@apexpay.com
Cc: Priya Singh <psingh@apexpay.com>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: production latency spike and 502s — urgent

Alex / Redwood team —

We started seeing a severe latency spike and a wave of 502s for our payments verification route around 08:45 PT. This is impacting live authorizations. Attached is a short CSV snapshot of request latencies and error counts from our side.

Two quick asks:
- Can you confirm what runtime logs you can pull for the timeframe 08:40–09:00 PT?
- Do your logs include request payloads or any customer-submitted sensitive fields? Our security team needs immediate clarity before we escalate further.

We have an executive call in 30 minutes — any quick answers appreciated.

—Jordan
VP Engineering, ApexPayments
[attachment: apexpay_latency_snapshot_20280220.csv (CSV, 48KB)]

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Jordan Clark <jordan.clark@apexpay.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Priya Singh <psingh@apexpay.com>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 09:22:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: production latency spike and 502s — urgent

Jordan —

Thanks for the heads-up and the CSV. We’ve opened an incident (INC-20280220-APX) and Marcus is on lead for the runtime traces. Quick answers up front:
- Yes, we can export request-level runtime traces for the timeframe you specified.
- By default our runtime traces do NOT persist customer payloads in plaintext. We record request metadata (route, model variant, token counts, latency, error codes) and limited hashed identifiers for correlation.

However, depending on your configuration (webhook body passthrough, custom functions) there are a few places where upstream payloads may have been forwarded into logs. We will do a scoped extraction and provide:
  1) a hashed/filtered trace export for 08:40–09:00 PT (no raw inputs)
  2) a redacted export if legal requires payloads for the audit — we’ll apply field-level redaction.

Marcus / Vanessa — please pull the trace export and a short timeline. Priya — please let us know any legal constraints from Apex on retention or redaction.

I’ll stay on the line during your exec call if helpful.

—Alex
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference — Production Systems
[internal note: incident INC-20280220-APX opened; priority=P1]

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Clark <jordan.clark@apexpay.com>, Priya Singh <psingh@apexpay.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 10:05:12 -08:00
Subject: Re: production latency spike and 502s — runtime trace and extraction

Team —

I pulled the runtime trace for 08:38–09:02 PT and started a filtered export. Summary of what I see:
- Spike origin: sustained token generation latency on model-root that caused upstream timeouts at our edge proxies.
- Error surface: 502 gateway errors, correlated with queueing delays in the GPU pool for the model variant pinned by Apex.
- Logged fields: request id, timestamp, route, model variant, seq_len, latency_ms, error_code, and a hashed request fingerprint.

What I DID NOT see in the runtime trace: raw request bodies or un-hashed user inputs. There is a separate <function-logs> stream for customer function outputs that can include payloads if customers opt-in; I checked Apex’s config and there’s no global opt-in flag set.

Attachments for your review:
- redwood_runtime_trace_20280220.json (filtered export) — contains no raw payloads, only metadata and hashed fingerprints.
- support_thread_screenshot.png — console view at time of spike.

Next steps I recommend:
1) Share the filtered export with Apex security/legal.
2) If Apex requests raw payloads for an audit, we need an explicit written request from Apex Legal (psingh@apexpay.com) specifying fields and lawful basis; we will then perform a controlled redaction export and log chain-of-custody.
3) Preserve the raw short-term buffer under legal hold while we validate config — I’ve toggled export protection on the incident so traces are immutable for 30 days.

—Marcus
Principal Reliability Engineer
[attachment: redwood_runtime_trace_20280220.json (JSON, 2.1MB)]

From: Priya Singh <psingh@apexpay.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 11:03:45 -08:00
Subject: Re: production latency spike and 502s — legal / evidence requirements

Thanks — that clarifies a lot. ApexPayments legal requests the following:
1) A timeboxed export (08:40–09:00 PT) that includes headers and metadata. Do not include raw cardholder or full personal data unless absolutely required; if you assert none exists, please state that in writing.
2) If any customer-submitted fields are present in the function logs, provide a field-level mapping and a redacted sample.
3) A short attestation that the exported artifact was pulled from immutable traces and the chain-of-custody (who pulled it, when, and how it was stored/transferred).

Please prepare the export and attestation. We will provide written approval if the scope is limited to metadata + redacted samples.

—Priya Singh
Senior Counsel, ApexPayments

[Forwarded note: Apex execs are on a 11:30 PT call — they requested a 1-paragraph summary for the board.]

From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
To: Priya Singh <psingh@apexpay.com>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 15:34:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: production latency spike and 502s — delivery and custody

Priya / Alex / Marcus —

We have completed the scoped export and redaction exercise:
- Export package: filtered-trace-apx-20280220.zip (contains redwood_runtime_trace_20280220.json with hashed fingerprints; sample_redacted_function_logs.csv with column-level redaction applied).
- Chain of custody: extracted by Marcus Lin at 2028-02-20T10:06:21-08:00 from immutable incident archive (INC-20280220-APX), staged in an access-restricted S3 bucket (apx-legal-exports) with object-level encryption and access logs enabled.
- Transfer: shared via a time-limited (7 day) secure drive link. No raw cardholder or clear-text PII present in the artifacts we exported.

Attached: support_thread_screenshot.png (console snapshot) and the drive link in the secure channel.

Jordan — we’ll join your exec call if you need us; summary for the board: brief root cause (GPU queueing -> increased generation latency -> upstream timeouts), mitigations in flight (scale GPU pool, fallback routing) and evidence package shared with legal.

Next actions:
- Ops: Monitor tail latency and confirm the autoscaler caught up.
- Legal: Confirm if Apex wants a deeper payload-level export (requires written approval and a scoped SOW).

—Vanessa Ortiz
Head of Customer Reliability, Redwood Inference
[attachment: support_thread_screenshot.png (PNG, 96KB)]

title: Physical boundary evidence and retention lifecycle for Dedicated region pin (Halcyon)
preview:
From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Sarah Li <sarah.li@halcyonfinance.com>, Timothy O'Neill <tim.oneill@halcyonfinance.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 14 Feb 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Region pinning artifacts and evidence handoff

Hi Sarah / Tim,

Thanks again for the conversation today. As discussed, Halcyon's Dedicated tenancy will be pinned to a single AWS region (us-west-2) and you asked for concrete artifacts we can provide to satisfy your compliance checklist around physical boundary, backup scope, and secure deletion.

What we'll provide (next 3 business days unless you ask for expedited delivery):
- An updated region pinning runbook (attached) that captures the tenancy binding, routing rules, and operational controls. (region_pinning_runbook_v2.pdf)
- Network topology diagram showing host aggregates & VPC boundaries (png attached).
- Tenant-level erase/wipe log produced after a test purge (we'll run a scrub and share CSV evidence).
- Snapshot/backup policy excerpt confirming cross-region replication settings and retention windows.

Quick clarifying questions from our side so we prepare exactly what you need:
1) For the tenant wipe evidence, do you need the raw event timestamps + operator id, or an HMAC-signed digest of the log?
2) Any specific retention window you want captured in the runbook (e.g., 7-day snapshots vs 30-day)?
3) Are there any additional third parties (auditors) we should copy on delivery?

I copied Vanessa and Jordan from our Platform Ops & Solutions teams; they'll coordinate the purge and generate the wipe artifact. We'll attach the runbook draft in this thread for your review.

Regards,
Marcus Lin
Solutions Engineering | Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.ai
+1 415-555-0182

Attachment: region_pinning_runbook_v2.pdf


From: Sarah Li <sarah.li@halcyonfinance.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Timothy O'Neill <tim.oneill@halcyonfinance.com>, Halcyon Security <security@halcyonfinance.com>
Date: Wed, 15 Feb 2028 11:04:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Region pinning artifacts and evidence handoff

Marcus —

Thanks, this covers most of what we need. A couple of quick asks before you run the purge:
- We prefer the wipe log to include both a machine-readable CSV (timestamps, resource ids, operator id) and an SHA256 digest file so we can archive it in our records.
- Snapshot/backup policy: please confirm that cross-region replication is disabled for Halcyon's tenancy and provide the policy excerpt in the runbook.
- Also confirm whether KMS keys for this tenancy are customer-managed or Redwood-managed, and the KMS region.

If you can include the CSV + hash and the runbook update, we can sign off from the security team and close the open item in HS-98765.

Appreciate the quick turnaround.

— Sarah Li
Head of Infrastructure Security | Halcyon Finance
sarah.li@halcyonfinance.com

Date: Fri, 17 Feb 2028 14:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Region pinning artifacts and evidence handoff — run, purge, and artifacts

Hi Sarah / Tim —

Following up with the items Marcus outlined. We executed a scrub/test purge on a staging copy of Halcyon's tenancy today and produced the artifacts you requested. Summary:

1) Region and isolation:
- Tenancy is anchored to us-west-2 (availability zone spread within that region).
- No compute or persistent volumes for this tenancy are provisioned outside us-west-2; ephemeral caches are ephemeral and cleared on termination.

2) Backups / replication:
- Cross-region replication is disabled for customer snapshots for this tenancy. Automatic snapshots are retained for 7 days by default (configurable per contract).
- KMS: Keys are customer-managed via your supplied KMS key (alias: alias/halcyon-dedicated-usw2). KMS region: us-west-2.

3) Wipe evidence delivered:
- tenant_wipe_log_HAL-20280217.csv (attached) — machine-readable rows with timestampUTC, resource_id, action, operator_id.
- tenant_wipe_log_HAL-20280217.sha256 — sha256 digest of the CSV (attached entry in drive link).
- network_topology_us-west-2.png (attached) — topology showing tenancy VPCs and host aggregates.

Operation notes and controls:
- The purge was run via our Dedicated orchestration path (no human access to raw disks). Operator id in the CSV is an internal ops service account id (svc-dedicated-wipe).
- For greater auditability we can provide a signed attest statement (timestamped) from our Platform Ops team; let us know if you want Redwood's attestation on letterhead.

Next steps we need from Halcyon to finalize the artifact package:
- Confirm you accept the CSV + hash as-is, or request additional fields.
- Confirm the retention window you want reflected in the runbook (we currently document 7-day snapshot retention).
- Indicate whether you need a formal attestation (PDF) or if the drive link + CSV+hash suffice.

Attachments: tenant_wipe_log_HAL-20280217.csv, region_pinning_runbook_v2.pdf (updated minor sections), network_topology_us-west-2.png

Thanks — happy to hop on a short call if you'd like to walk the artifacts.

— Vanessa Ortiz
Platform Operations | Redwood Inference
vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai
