title: Post-demo: SLO action matrix & gating questions
preview:
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
To: Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@brightbeam.com>
Cc: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 09 Jan 2028 15:22:00 -0800
Subject: Post-demo: SLO action matrix & gating questions

Thanks again for taking the demo today, Oliver — really appreciated the candid discussion. I wanted to capture the performance items we agreed to verify and call out the remaining gating questions so we have a clean MAP (mutual action plan).

High-level asks (from Brightbeam):
- 95th P50 latency target for typical single-turn inference: <= 120ms (text-gen, 512 tokens)
- Sustained throughput: 200 qps peak for batch endpoint, burst to 400 qps for short windows
- Concurrent live sessions for streaming use-case: target 120 concurrent streams with sub-200ms first-byte latency
- KV/prefix caching must be supported for repeated user context (expected 70% reuse)

What we (Redwood) showed and next validation steps:
- Demo highlights: streaming demo with client-side latency ~170ms median (recorded), autoscaling policy spun an additional worker at +200 qps
- Action: Share sample perf logs + demo recording (attached).
- Action: Run a short pilot with production-ish traces (we requested their representative workload — Brightbeam to provide sample traces by EOD Tue).

Open technical gating questions for Brightbeam to confirm:
1) Exact token distribution for the 512-token scenario (how often do they hit 512 vs 128 vs 32?)
2) Allowed tail latency for first-byte on streaming (is 200ms strict or a guideline?)
3) Data residency / audit requirements for the pilot (which region + retention)

Proposed timeline (subject to your trace delivery):
- Tue (01/10): Brightbeam shares anonymized traces + sample payloads
- Wed–Thu: Redwood runs pilot (2 days) and sends perf artifact (logs + replay summary)
- Fri: Joint call to review SLO checklist and decision criteria

Owners:
- Aisha Rahman (Redwood AE) — commercial & timeline
- Camila Reyes (SE) — pilot config and validation
- Marcus Lin (Solutions Eng) — log analysis and topology suggestions

Attachments: demo-recording-20280109.mp4 (30m), perf-logs-sample.json.gz, proposed_slo_checklist.xlsx

Short ask: Please confirm token distribution and whether 95th-latency at <= 120ms is a hard requirement or negotiable for a 30-day SLA. If you can share the traces tomorrow we’ll prioritize the pilot.

Thanks —
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aisha@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123

Redwood Inference — Production-first inference for LLMs


From: Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@brightbeam.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Shah <priya.shah@brightbeam.com>, contracts@brightbeam.com
Date: Tue, 10 Jan 2028 10:18:00 -0500
Subject: Re: Post-demo: SLO action matrix & gating questions

Thanks, Aisha — great recap and sensible plan. A few clarifications from our side after internal sync: we can provide representative traces by EOD tomorrow (they will be anonymized). Quick answers to your gating questions below.

1) Token distribution: roughly 60% are short queries (~16–48 tokens), 30% are mid (~128 tokens), 10% hit 512 tokens. We can surface a small sample with the traces.
2) Streaming first-byte latency: 200ms is our target, but we can be flexible to 250ms during pilot to allow for environment variance. For GA we’d like to tighten to <=200ms.
3) Data residency: pilot can run in us-west-2. We need audit logs for request/response hashes retained for 90 days; full payloads cannot be stored by Redwood unless explicitly allowed — hashed evidence is fine.

Two additional asks:
- Can you confirm TLS 1.2+ and KMS integration for keys used in private deployments?
- We have a standard security addendum — I’ve CC’d contracts. Priya will follow up if we need a custom PO.

We’re aligned on the timeline; we’ll send traces tomorrow and would like the pilot replay results by Thursday. Also please share the runbook / checklist you’ll use so our SRE can follow the same verification steps.

Oliver Grant
Platform Engineering Lead, Brightbeam Health
oliver.grant@brightbeam.com | +1 (617) 555-0987


From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@brightbeam.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Jan 2028 08:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-demo: SLO action matrix & gating questions

Thanks Oliver — that token distribution is really helpful. Quick technical notes from our side ahead of running the pilot: 

- Pilot config: we’ll run two modes (1) replay of provided traces against your baseline model hosted on Redwood Dedicated, and (2) a streaming emulation with 120 concurrent streams for 30 minutes to observe first-byte and sustained tail behavior.
- Autoscaling policy: we’ll use a scale threshold of 180 qps sustained for 60s with a 3-worker max surge for burst windows. We’ll share exact instance/worker types in the runbook.
- Caching: our prefix KV caching can be enabled; based on your 70% reuse estimate we expect ~40% token-cost reduction in replay. We’ll include cache hit breakdown in the perf artifact.
- Security: TLS 1.2+ supported; KMS integrations available. For the pilot we will only store hashed request identifiers and aggregated metrics (no plaintext payloads) per your note.

Deliverables we’ll provide after the 48h run:
- Replay perf logs + percentile breakdowns (p50/p95/p99)
- Streaming session traces with first-byte and payload latencies
- Cache hit stats and token-cost delta estimate
- Recommended config for GA (batching/quantization suggestions)

SRE runbook (short):
- Step 1: Upload traces to shared Drive folder (we’ll send link)
- Step 2: Redwood runs replay + streaming emulation in us-west-2
- Step 3: Camila reviews raw logs with Marcus and posts summary to HubSpot record
- Step 4: Joint review call to go over acceptance criteria (we propose Fri 01/12 10:00 PT)

Attaching a concise checklist (slo-checklist.xlsx) with the exact measurement windows we’ll use.

—Camila Reyes
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.com


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
To: Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@brightbeam.com>
Cc: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, contracts@brightbeam.com
Date: Thu, 12 Jan 2028 16:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-demo: SLO action matrix & gating questions

Perfect — thanks for the fast turnaround and clarifications, Oliver. Confirming the MAP and owners below (final items before pilot kickoff).

Pilot kickoff checklist (owners & due dates):
- Brightbeam: deliver anonymized traces and token distribution samples (Oliver / Priya) — due Tue 01/10 EOD (noted you’ll deliver tomorrow)
- Redwood: run 48h replay + 30m streaming emulation in us-west-2 (Camila / Marcus) — Wed–Thu 01/11–01/12
- Redwood: deliver perf artifact (logs + summary) and cost delta estimate (Camila) — Thu 01/12 by 18:00 PT
- Joint review: Fri 01/13 10:00 PT (proposed) — confirm attendee list
- Contracts: Priya / contracts@brightbeam.com to share any SAs or SDA items; Aisha to coordinate commercial terms and PO (Aisha)

Decision criteria we’ll use on review call:
- Functional: p95 <= 120–250ms (pilot tolerance window: 250ms); p99 visibility and acceptable rollback plan if p99 exceeds threshold by >2x
- Throughput: sustain 200 qps with ability to burst to 400 qps for at least 1–2 minute windows without request failures >0.1%
- Streaming: first-byte median <200ms, pilot allowance to 250ms; must show stable connection handoff under 120 concurrent streams
- Security/compliance: hashed request evidence + 90-day audit log retention and TLS 1.2+/KMS confirmation

Next steps (today): I’ll upload the runbook + demo recording to the shared Drive and create the HubSpot task (link included). Marcus — please prepare any topology diagrams you want me to include in the shared folder.

If this all looks good I’ll ping Priya to begin the contracts thread and we’ll target the joint review on Friday.

Cheers,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aisha@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123

--

—Camila Reyes

camila@redwood.com


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
To: Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@brightbeam.com>
Cc: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, contracts@brightbeam.com
Date: Thu, 12 Jan 2028 16:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-demo: SLO action matrix & gating questions

Perfect — thanks for the fast turnaround and clarifications, Oliver. Confirming the MAP and owners below (final items before pilot kickoff).

Pilot kickoff checklist (owners & due dates):
- Brightbeam: deliver anonymized traces and token distribution samples (Oliver / Priya) — due Tue 01/10 EOD (noted you’ll deliver tomorrow)
- Redwood: run 48h replay + 30m streaming emulation in us-west-2 (Camila / Marcus) — Wed–Thu 01/11–01/12
- Redwood: deliver perf artifact (logs + summary) and cost delta estimate (Camila) — Thu 01/12 by 18:00 PT
- Joint review: Fri 01/13 10:00 PT (proposed) — confirm attendee list
- Contracts: Priya / contracts@brightbeam.com to share any SAs or SDA items; Aisha to coordinate commercial terms and PO (Aisha)

Decision criteria we’ll use on review call:
- Functional: p95 <= 120–250ms (pilot tolerance window: 250ms); p99 visibility and acceptable rollback plan if p99 exceeds threshold by >2x
- Throughput: sustain 200 qps with ability to burst to 400 qps for at least 1–2 minute windows without request failures >0.1%
- Streaming: first-byte median <200ms, pilot allowance to 250ms; must show stable connection handoff under 120 concurrent streams
- Security/compliance: hashed request evidence + 90-day audit log retention and TLS 1.2+/KMS confirmation

Next steps (today): I’ll upload the runbook + demo recording to the shared Drive and create the HubSpot task (link included). Marcus — please prepare any topology diagrams you want me to include in the shared folder.

If this all looks good I’ll ping Priya to begin the contracts thread and we’ll target the joint review on Friday.

Cheers,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

title: Cobalt Systems — Private Deployment Integration Playbook & Ops Handoff
preview:
From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Lena Park <lena.park@cobaltsys.com>, Tom Harris <tharris@cobaltsys.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 30 Nov 2027 16:12:00 -0800
Subject: Cobalt Systems — Private Deployment Integration Playbook & Ops Handoff

Hi Lena / Tom —

As discussed during last week's kickoff, I'm moving the implementation handoff into motion for the Private deployment (VPC peering + private endpoints + on-prem package). Attached is the first cut of the integration playbook we will use as the single source of truth during the implementation. Key items included: a mutual action plan, required network artifacts, firewall / ACL expectations, on‑prem installer checklist, and a proposed staging schedule.

Attachments (attached to this thread):
- Cobalt-Integration-Playbook-v1.2.pdf (PDF, 42 pages)
- vpc-peering-template.yml (YAML, sample peering configuration)
- onprem-install-checklist.xlsx (Excel, installer runbook and OS/preflight checks)

Immediate asks:
- Can you confirm your preferred maintenance window for on‑prem install (weeknights vs weekend)?
- Please provide the IP range you plan to use for the private endpoints and whether you will NAT behind a firewall or allow direct routing to our private IPs.
- If you have an internal ticket or RFC for VPC peering, drop the ID and we'll align our network team.

Proposed next step: 60‑minute alignment call to walk the playbook and agree on the network cutover/test plan. I've tentatively proposed Fri 12/3 14:00 PST — if that works I will send the invite.

I'll be coordinating the customer success and implementation activities. Monica will own data prep and acceptance criteria; Priyom will be the lead for the runtime deployment (on‑prem agent & installer); Marcus is our network SME and will be on the peering/BGP items. Grace is the CS lead for escalation and success metrics.

Thanks — looking forward to getting this staged.

—
Avery Johnson
Customer Success Manager | Redwood Inference
avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com | +1 415 555 0123



From: Lena Park <lena.park@cobaltsys.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Tom Harris <tharris@cobaltsys.com>, eng-infra@cobaltsys.com
Date: Wed, 01 Dec 2027 09:23:00 -0500
Subject: Re: Cobalt Systems — Private Deployment Integration Playbook & Ops Handoff

Thanks Avery —

Appreciate the clear playbook. Quick responses to your asks:
- Preferred maintenance window: weekend overnight (Sat 01:00–05:00 local) — lower traffic for our batch jobs.
- Private endpoints: we plan to allocate 10.20.32.0/24 for the private endpoints. We will NAT outgoing traffic through our perimeter firewall (no direct public routing).
- VPC peer ticket: INFRA-7821 (I'll invite the infra ticket into the thread).

A couple of questions after a quick skim:
1) For the on‑prem installer, does Redwood require inbound access from your control plane to the host, or can all communication be established outbound over TLS from our side?
2) The playbook references 'BGP ASN exchange' — are you expecting us to run BGP for failover, or is a static route + health probes sufficient for initial cutover?

We can jump on the proposed 12/3 slot; please send a calendar invite and a short agenda. Also — we need an updated SLA draft with the Private deployment metrics for our procurement team (I saw the DocSend link in related links; can you attach a redlined version for procurement?).

Cheers,
Lena Park
Infrastructure Program Manager
Cobalt Systems

> [original message attached]



From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Lena Park <lena.park@cobaltsys.com>, Tom Harris <tharris@cobaltsys.com>
Date: Wed, 01 Dec 2027 11:02:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Cobalt Systems — Private Deployment Integration Playbook & Ops Handoff

Folks — looping in the infra details we need to finalize before the on‑prem installer run. Quick notes from the Redwood side: 

- Control plane connectivity: Our on‑prem agent establishes outbound TLS to our control plane; we do not require inbound open ports for the default installer flow. If Cobalt prefers an inbound management plane, we can support it but it requires an exception in the firewall (see playbook section 4.2).
- Private endpoints: 10.20.32.0/24 is fine; please confirm there are no overlapping prefixes with your staging environment.
- NAT vs direct routes: NAT outbound is supported; for KV/prefix cache efficiency we recommend direct private routing where possible but we'll work with NAT.
- BGP: For the initial cutover a static route + health probes is sufficient. If you want accelerated cross-region failover later, we recommend a later BGP step (we can run BGP peering with ASN exchange).

Attachment: vpc-peering-template.yml (sample BGP/static configuration) — we'll provide a filled example during the call.

Action items I propose before the call: 
1) Cobalt to confirm the exact host(s) where the on‑prem installer will run and provide OS images / kernel versions.
2) Cobalt to confirm whether the installer host will sit behind a NAT gateway.
3) Redwood to prepare two network diagrams (static-route flow; BGP flow) and one example peering config to share. Marcus will lead that.

I'll be ready to walk through the runtime install steps and failure remediation during the alignment call.

—
Priyom Das
Senior Implementation Engineer | Redwood Inference
priyom.das@redwoodinference.com



From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Lena Park <lena.park@cobaltsys.com>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 02 Dec 2027 09:15:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Cobalt Systems — Private Deployment Integration Playbook & Ops Handoff

Hi Lena —

Thanks for the details. For the peering work (INFRA-7821) we'll need the following from you ahead of the call: 
- Confirm public ASN you want to advertise (if you plan to use BGP). If not, confirm the static route IPs and next hop.
- Any firewall ACLs that will block AWS/GCP peering traffic (we usually need TCP/UDP ephemeral open for certain health checks).
- Expected test IP we can ping from Redwood staging to validate route propagation.

For now, we'll assume static routes for the first pass and document BGP as the follow-on. Priyom: I'll prepare the 'static route' diagram and the quick test checklist (ICMP and TCP port 443 probe).

Lena, if the weekend window is Sat 01:00–05:00 local, can you confirm timezone (is that PST or EST for Cobalt infra?) — I want to make sure we schedule our oncall engineers correctly.

Regards,
Marcus Lin
Network Architect | Redwood Inference

title: FY28 target-setting sprint — timeline & risk scenarios
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Sun, 07 Nov 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: FY28 target-setting sprint — timeline & risk scenarios

Team —

As discussed in last week's leadership sync, it's time to run a short, focused planning sprint to: 1) land FY28 targets by org, 2) run two downside/upside scenarios for revenue and capacity, and 3) produce a QBR-ready one-pager for exec review.

Proposed cadence (week-by-week):
- Week 1 (Nov 8–12): Data pull + baseline targets from each function (revenue ops, product, infra).
- Week 2 (Nov 15–19): Scenario modeling (base / conservative / upside) + cost levers.
- Week 3 (Nov 22–26): Consolidation, QBR slide deck draft, leadership review.

Ask: please confirm owners for the baseline deliverables by EOD Monday Nov 8. I've attached a scenario workbook that contains templates for revenue curves, headcount sensitivity, and cost-per-token levers.

Meeting: I'll schedule a 45m planning kickoff for Tue Nov 9 — calendar invite to follow.

Attachments: [Attachment: FY28_scenario_workbook.xlsx (xlsx, 1.1MB)]

Thanks,
Aditya Rao
Director, Revenue Operations
Redwood Inference

Subject: Re: FY28 target-setting sprint — timeline & risk scenarios

I'll flag any dependencies by EOD Monday.

title: Post-demo: SDK auth + JSON failover strategy (next steps)
preview:
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Harris <alex.harris@crestwave.ai>, Sasha Lim <sasha.lim@crestwave.ai>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2027 15:22:00 -0700
Subject: Post-demo: SDK auth + JSON failover strategy (next steps)

Hi Alex / Sasha — thanks again for the demo today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we keep momentum: 

1) Scope / outcomes we agreed
- 2-week POC: embeddings + re-ranker + structured output for search result normalization
- Success criteria: F1 > 0.78 on your labeled 5k queries; <200ms median gen latency for short responses

2) Integration items we need to action (we can split between infra and app):
- SDK/setup: add redwood-js v2.1.0 into backend service; we recommend server-side token flow (short-lived key + rotation)
- Auth: service-to-service JWT + per-route token scope for model inference (discussed audit logs requirement)
- Routing: prefer region us-west for latency; fallback policy to cheaper variant when load > 85%
- Structured output: agreed JSON envelope for results + explicit schema for \"hits\" array
- Validation + retry: schema validation at the SDK layer, automated constrained decoding with token cutoff + deterministic retry policy for malformed JSON

3) Ask from CrestWave (items you promised to provide)
- Sample dataset: 5k anonymized query/ground-truth pairs (can be a csv)
- List of production endpoints and expected QPS for sizing

Attachments: redwood-integration-checklist.xlsx; auth-routing-diagram.png; schema-fallbacks.md

From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Harris <alex.harris@crestwave.ai>, Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.ai>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 16 Sep 2027 11:37:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: SDK auth + JSON failover strategy (next steps)

Alex / team — copying the tech details you asked for so we can scope the POC precisely. High-level approach we've used successfully elsewhere: 

1) Auth & audit logs
- For POC: server-side short-lived API key (30m) exchanged via an internal auth service. Production: recommend Dedicated or Private with KMS-backed signing.
- Audit: per-request trace id + event stream; retention for Dedicated is configurable (30/90/365 days). For Private we can retain per your KMS policy — we support exportable log bundles for SOC evidence.

2) SDK/setup and region routing
- Use redwood-js v2.1.0 (node >=16). Example usage to call embedding + reranker endpoints: 
  const Redwood = require('@redwood/inference');
  const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_KEY, region: 'us-west' });

  // embeddings
  const emb = await client.embeddings.create({ model: 'rw-embed-2', input: query });

- Routing: implement primary route to us-west and a lower-cost fallback model (rw-compact-1) that is compatible for re-ranking when primary is saturated. SDK supports routePolicy: 'latency' | 'cost' in the client config.

3) Structured output reliability (JSON schema + constrained decoding)
- Envelope: { "request_id": "", "hits": [ {"id":"","score":0.0,"metadata":{} } ], "model_version": "" }
- Schema validation: we recommend validating the envelope at the SDK boundary using ajv (json-schema). If validation fails, two options controlled by a flag: 
  a) fail-fast: return error to caller (useful for strict pipelines)
  b) best-effort: attempt automatic repairs:
     - run a constrained-decode pass (temperature=0.0, max_new_tokens small) to coerce output into schema
     - if still invalid, fallback to cached response or try the compact model (rw-compact-1)
- Retry policy: idempotency key = request_id. Retries only for transient server errors (5xx) or for deterministic repair attempts; do NOT automatically retry on 4xx validation failures unless caller explicitly opts-in.

4) Embeddings & reranker notes
- Use batch embeddings with size 64 for latency/cost balance; store vector ids and a short metadata blob.
- Reranker should accept candidate ids and return normalized scores + optional structured corrections. We recommend an integration test that verifies stable reranker ordering on your 500 sample queries before full indexing.

5) Implementation checklist (what we'll deliver for POC)
- Sandbox project + API key (expires 30d)
- Sample SDK integration PR with schema validation + retry hooks
- Runbook: how to interpret failed JSON + mitigation steps (repair -> fallback -> manual review)

Snippet: sample constrained-decode call (pseudo):
  // server-side repair attempt
  const repaired = await client.completions.create({
    model: 'rw-general-1',
    prompt: promptForSchema(envelopeSchema),
    temperature: 0.0,
    max_new_tokens: 64,
    stop: ['\
\
']
  });

I'll prepare the SDK PR and a short demo script for the kickoff. If you can share the sample CSV and your expected QPS windows, we can size Dedicated.

Thanks,
Dev Patel
Senior Software Engineer, Platform Integrations | Redwood Inference

Hi Aisha,

Thanks again for walking us through Redwood's Dedicated offering last week. As we finalize procurement docs for the enterprise contract, our execs have asked for a formal latency commitment for the production workload. High level:

- Traffic profile: 1k QPS peak (text generation, average prompt length ~180 tokens)
- Request: p95 generation latency <= 90ms and overall service uptime >= 99.95% for US region
- Timeline: goal to sign and go live by Oct 1; willing to discuss a staged ramp/commitment if needed

We attached an updated proposal with the commercial numbers and tentative traffic ramp. Can Redwood confirm whether you can put a sub-90ms p95 latency commitment into an appendix for Neuronix's SLA? Cc: 
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2027 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Latency commitment request for enterprise contract

Team — forwarding Ellis's ask from Neuronix (external). They're requesting p95 <= 90ms at ~1k QPS and 99.95% uptime for US. They want an appendix in the contract.

Quick asks:
- Product/engineering: feasibility and minimal technical constraints we would need to guarantee p95<=90ms (dedicated pool, model variant, quantization, traffic shaping).
- SRE/ops: runbook and operational controls, how we'd measure/verify latency for SLA reporting; what monitoring + synthetic tests are required.
- Sales: commercial levers (dedicated premium, capacity reservation, penalties). Marcus, please advise on what we can offer commercially while protecting ops.

Timing: Neuronix is aiming to sign by Oct 1. Can I get an internal summary from each of you by EOD Wednesday (Sept 1)? If we push back, I'll propose an alternative SLA structure (e.g., p95 <= 120ms standard + a paid low-latency add-on).

Thanks — Aisha

--
Aisha Rahman
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aisha_rahman@redwood.com


From: Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 31 Aug 2027 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Latency commitment request for enterprise contract

Hi all,

Quick technical read: sub-90ms p95 at 1k QPS for generation is extremely aggressive unless we constrain a few dimensions. High level feasibility and trade-offs below.

What makes sub-90ms hard:
- Model choice & context length: larger models and longer prompts add variance.
- Cold vs warm state: LRU cache misses and KV cache population cause spikes.
- Hardware contention: multi-tenant GPU pools introduce jitter.

Feasible approaches (in descending order of operational cost):
1) Dedicated reserved pool (recommended path)
   - Dedicated mulligan: provision N GPUs exclusively for Neuronix traffic + pre-warmed KV cache and pinned model weights (hot cache)
   - Expected p95: <=90ms for typical prompts if we limit sequence length to <=256 tokens and use the quantized low-latency model variant (we have validated traces for similar customers).
   - Trade-offs: ~2.1x incremental hosting cost vs shared hosted; needs a committed monthly baseline (we should quantify in $).

2) Traffic routing + hybrid fallback
   - Route 60-70% of traffic to low-latency variant on dedicated nodes; overflow to standard nodes with p95<=140ms.
   - Pros: lower cost than full dedicated. Cons: complexity + degraded experience on overflow.

3) Contract-level constraints rather than strict number
   - Offer p95<=120ms as a guaranteed SLA and an optional low-latency premium add-on for p95<=90ms tied to a dedicated pool.

Operational requirements (minimum):
- SLO definition doc: exact measurement windows, warmup handling, synthetic traffic spec.
- Canary and staged rollouts for model version changes.
- Capacity reservation + autoscaling guardrails.

Engineering effort estimates: small-medium changes to provisioning + SLA telemetry (~3-4 weeks of infra/product effort to add formal metrics + canary jobs and automate warm pools), plus SRE runbook (~1 week). Cost delta: estimate 1.8x-2.5x depending on GPU class and replication.

Attachment: [latency-analysis-2027-08.xlsx] (summary in body above)

Happy to jump on a technical call with Neuronix to align constraints.

— Dev
Dev Patel
Senior Product Manager, Serving Runtime
dev_patel@redwood.com


From: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 01 Sep 2027 11:31:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Latency commitment request for enterprise contract

Adding ops POV.

If we go the dedicated-pool route we will need the following before we can sign any hard SLA:
- Definitive SLO language (measurement method, windowing, exclusion policies for maintenance and force majeure).
- A synthetic workload and monitoring dashboard we control and the customer acknowledges as the source of truth for SLA measurement.
- A runbook covering: capacity pre-warm, cache warming guidance, incident escalation, and RTO/RPO for model reloads.
- On-call staffing commitments for high-touch accounts (this increases our OPEX and needs to be priced).

Operational risks and mitigations:
- Deployment & model-change regressions: require canary for every model update and an automatic fallback to a compatible model variant if latency degrades.
- Region-level failures: consider cross-region failover but exclude it from the latency guarantee (we can offer uptime as cross-region).

I will draft a short SLO + runbook template for Neuronix and share by EOD Thu. Also note we need legal to vet penalty language (Marcus to loop in Legal).

Attachment: [slo-runbook-draft.md]

— Grace
Grace O'Connor
Head of SRE, Redwood Inference


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Ellis Thompson <ellis@neuronix.ai>, contracts@neuronix.ai
Cc: Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 02 Sep 2027 16:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Latency commitment request for enterprise contract

Hi Ellis — thanks for the ask and for sending the proposal. Internal alignment complete; below is a summary of what we can offer + next steps.

Proposed approach
- Baseline SLA (no additional charge): 99.9% uptime SLA (region-level), and p95 generation latency <= 120ms measured on Redwood synthetic suite during business hours. This is supported on standard Dedicated offering.

- Low-latency premium (add-on): p95 <= 90ms guaranteed for traffic that routes to a Neuronix-dedicated pool with the following constraints:
  - Dedicated capacity reservation (monthly commit) sized to expected peak (we'll propose numbers after you share 30/60/90 day ramp)
  - Prompt length capped at 256 tokens for guaranteed measurement; larger prompts will be excluded from the latency calculation
  - Canary + synthetic testing required for each model change; automatic fallback policy for regressions
  - Pricing uplift: estimate ~2.0x cost multiplier vs shared Dedicated (final pricing to be reflected in appendix)

Operational & legal items
- We'll provide a draft SLO/runbook (Grace) and a measurement appendix for the contract.
- Legal to review penalty language; Marcus will coordinate with enterprise legal on acceptable terms.

Next steps
1) Aisha: send one-page commercial appendix with estimated committed capacity and price band (ASAP). [I will include a DocuSign link for the add-on if Neuronix accepts the premium model.]
2) Neuronix: confirm expected ramp (30/60/90 day traffic numbers) so we can size dedicated pool.
3) Dev & Grace: finalize SLO + synthetic test spec and present to the customer in a technical call.

If that sounds reasonable we can put the low-latency add-on into an appendix for contract signature and keep the baseline SLA in the main agreement. Marcus, please advise on any commercial guardrails.

Thanks — looking forward to next steps.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Team — forwarding Ellis's ask from Neuronix (external). They're requesting p95 <= 90ms at ~1k QPS and 99.95% uptime for US. They want an appendix in the contract.

Quick asks:
- Product/engineering: feasibility and minimal technical constraints we would need to guarantee p95<=90ms (dedicated pool, model variant, quantization, traffic shaping).
- SRE/ops: runbook and operational controls, how we'd measure/verify latency for SLA reporting; what monitoring + synthetic tests are required.
- Sales: commercial levers (dedicated premium, capacity reservation, penalties). Marcus, please advise on what we can offer commercially while protecting ops.

Timing: Neuronix is aiming to sign by Oct 1. Can I get an internal summary from each of you by EOD Wednesday (Sept 1)? If we push back, I'll propose an alternative SLA structure (e.g., p95 <= 120ms standard + a paid low-latency add-on).

aisha_rahman@redwood.com


From: Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 31 Aug 2027 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Latency commitment request for enterprise contract

Hi all,

Quick technical read: sub-90ms p95 at 1k QPS for generation is extremely aggressive unless we constrain a few dimensions. High level feasibility and trade-offs below.

What makes sub-90ms hard:
- Model choice & context length: larger models and longer prompts add variance.
- Cold vs warm state: LRU cache misses and KV cache population cause spikes.
- Hardware contention: multi-tenant GPU pools introduce jitter.

Feasible approaches (in descending order of operational cost):
1) Dedicated reserved pool (recommended path)
   - Dedicated mulligan: provision N GPUs exclusively for Neuronix traffic + pre-warmed KV cache and pinned model weights (hot cache)
   - Expected p95: <=90ms for typical prompts if we limit sequence length to <=256 tokens and use the quantized low-latency model variant (we have validated traces for similar customers).
   - Trade-offs: ~2.1x incremental hosting cost vs shared hosted; needs a committed monthly baseline (we should quantify in $).

2) Traffic routing + hybrid fallback
   - Route 60-70% of traffic to low-latency variant on dedicated nodes; overflow to standard nodes with p95<=140ms.
   - Pros: lower cost than full dedicated. Cons: complexity + degraded experience on overflow.

3) Contract-level constraints rather than strict number
   - Offer p95<=120ms as a guaranteed SLA and an optional low-latency premium add-on for p95<=90ms tied to a dedicated pool.

Operational requirements (minimum):
- SLO definition doc: exact measurement windows, warmup handling, synthetic traffic spec.
- Canary and staged rollouts for model version changes.
- Capacity reservation + autoscaling guardrails.

Engineering effort estimates: small-medium changes to provisioning + SLA telemetry (~3-4 weeks of infra/product effort to add formal metrics + canary jobs and automate warm pools), plus SRE runbook (~1 week). Cost delta: estimate 1.8x-2.5x depending on GPU class and replication.

Attachment: [latency-analysis-2027-08.xlsx] (summary in body above)

Happy to jump on a technical call with Neuronix to align constraints.

— Dev
Dev Patel
Senior Product Manager, Serving Runtime
dev_patel@redwood.com


From: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 01 Sep 2027 11:31:00 -0700

Adding ops POV.

If we go the dedicated-pool route we will need the following before we can sign any hard SLA:
- Definitive SLO language (measurement method, windowing, exclusion policies for maintenance and force majeure).
- A synthetic workload and monitoring dashboard we control and the customer acknowledges as the source of truth for SLA measurement.
- A runbook covering: capacity pre-warm, cache warming guidance, incident escalation, and RTO/RPO for model reloads.
- On-call staffing commitments for high-touch accounts (this increases our OPEX and needs to be priced).

Operational risks and mitigations:
- Deployment & model-change regressions: require canary for every model update and an automatic fallback to a compatible model variant if latency degrades.
- Region-level failures: consider cross-region failover but exclude it from the latency guarantee (we can offer uptime as cross-region).

I will draft a short SLO + runbook template for Neuronix and share by EOD Thu. Also note we need legal to vet penalty language (Marcus to loop in Legal).

Attachment: [slo-runbook-draft.md]

— Grace
Grace O'Connor

To: Lisa Chen <lisa.chen@acmehealth.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2027 08:22:40 -0700
Subject: Re: Data Processing Annex - initial evidence & subprocessor list (privacy counsel comments)

Hi Lisa / Aisha,

Thanks — I reviewed the Acme template and Aisha's redlines. High-level counsel comments below (inline redlines in PDF):

1) Transfers: We will implement SCCs as the lawful mechanism. I propose a short EU Addendum that:
   - Declares SCCs as the default transfer mechanism
   - Confirms Redwood's commitments on backup residency and responsible subprocessors for EU customer data
   - Includes a 30-day notice period for adding subprocessors with a material change threshold (security or location).

2) Subprocessor inventory: The CSV is complete for our primary hosted stack, but note: the inventory flags two categories — "managed partners" (used by Dedicated deployments) and "transient vendors" (cache/CDN providers that we rely on across regions). For the latter, we provide control evidence rather than per-tenant contracts. If Acme requires per-subprocessor contracts we can discuss carveouts for Dedicated deployment only.

3) Liability & caps: Your template requests unlimited liability for certain data incidents. Our position is to keep a contractual cap tied to fees (standard market practice). I'm proposing a compromise: a raised liability cap specifically for privacy breaches tied to a multiple of annual fees for the first 12 months after breach discovery. Details in PDF.

4) Retention & deletion: We need clearer language on what Acme expects for backups and cold archives. Redwood's hosted offering maintains encrypted backups across regions for 90 days by default; Dedicated customers can request custom retention. Suggest we add a short schedule to Annex A for retention defaults and options.

5) Evidence handoff: SOC2 summary attached. For deeper evidence (control narratives, penetration testing artifacts) we will provide access via a secure drive link or NDA-protected exchange. Please confirm which route counsel prefers.

Next steps I suggest: a brief 30-minute call with Acme counsel to confirm (a) transfer mechanism + addendum scope, and (b) whether per-subprocessor contracts are required for their procurement team. Aisha / Marcus — can you propose 30m slots Tues 08/31 10–12 PT or Wed 09/01 13–16 PT?

Best regards,
Grace Oconnor
Privacy Counsel, Redwood Inference

title: Dedicated capacity request — geo-hybrid pool sizing for Helios AI
preview:
From: Elena Torres <elena@helios.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@helios.ai>
Date: 2027-08-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Initial capacity ask — global footprint

Hi Alex,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Helios is moving to an enterprise contract and we need Redwood to support a geo-hybrid dedicated footprint (US + EU + APAC) to meet latency and data locality requirements. High level asks: 1) recommended reserved pool sizing per region for our expected traffic profile, 2) burst/backstop strategy for unpredictable model spikes, 3) onboarding timeline for APAC (we're targeting a Singapore pilot).

I've attached a spreadsheet with estimated QPS/peak hours per model and the mix of models (2x large-chat, 1x embedding-heavy, 1x reranker). We can be flexible with quantization if it helps reduce capacity needs — want your recommendation. Procurement looped in on timeline and PO cadence.

Can we get a preliminary sizing and a short proposal this week? We're aiming to sign by 08/20 if numbers align.

Thanks,
Elena
VP Product, Helios AI
--Attachment: Helios_requirements.xlsx (spreadsheet)--

From: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Torres <elena@helios.ai>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-08-01T12:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Initial capacity ask — global footprint

Hi Elena — thanks for the detail and the spreadsheet. I've looped in Lauren (sales lead), Marcus (capacity planning) and Hannah (APAC ops) so we can produce a working sizing and timeline. Quick follow-ups so our calc matches your goals:

- Can you confirm 99th percentile latency SLOs for chat vs embeddings (target ms)?
- Do you expect traffic to be stable cross-region (e.g., 40/35/25 split) or concentrated in certain markets during business hours?
- Any regulatory data residency constraints per model (EU-only, APAC-only)?
- Are you open to a staged rollout (reserve smaller initial pool + burst lanes) or do you need full reserved capacity at sign?

I propose Marcus and I turn around a preliminary region-by-region sizing today with three scenarios (conservative, baseline, lean) and a suggested sprint plan for APAC onboarding. Can we carve 30 minutes tomorrow to walk through?

Best,
Alex Martinez
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
--Attachment: (none)--

From: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-08-02T08:22:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Re: Initial capacity ask — global footprint

Team — forwarding Helios thread. I drafted a quick calc (attached) for our baseline scenario based on Elena's sheet. Key assumptions are in-line: 60% peak concurrency on chat models, 25% embeddings load, 15% reranker/batch jobs. I'm flagging two decision points for the customer: allow 4-bit/8-bit quantization for the chat family, and confirm APAC pilot node class (A100 vs L40s). If customer rejects quantization we need ~30% more reserved GPUs.

Please review Marcus — especially the APAC lead times (Hannah) and put together a recommended pool split (US/EU/APAC) and burst lane plan.

--Attachment: prelim_pool_calc.csv (CSV) --

From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>, Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-08-02T11:40:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Re: Initial capacity ask — global footprint

On Tue, Aug 2, 2027 at 08:22 AM Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Team — forwarding Helios thread. I drafted a quick calc (attached) for our baseline scenario based on Elena's sheet.

Thanks — ran three scenarios and here's our recommendation (numbers are reserved GPU-equivalents using our L40s profile):

- Baseline (preferred for signing): US 36, EU 24, APAC 18 reserved; burst lanes: 25% on top (auto-burst to shared pool + queued fallback).
- Conservative: US 50, EU 34, APAC 26 (for hard-SLA workloads; adds ~28% monthly cost).
- Lean (pilot-first): US 22, EU 14, APAC 8 (start small, 12-week ramp triggered by traffic thresholds).

Tradeoffs:
- Quantization to 8-bit reduces reserved GPUs by ~18%; 4-bit is aggressive but viable for some chat flows.
- APAC lead time for dedicated nodes in Singapore with our partner is ~6–8 weeks to provision + validation; can be shortened to 3–4 weeks with accelerated on-prem staging and prioritization (one-time onboarding surcharge).
- Recommend an initial contractual commitment for baseline with a 12-week canary in APAC to validate live traffic. After that, auto-scale policies can handle burst.

Links: sizing workbook + cost lines -> https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aB2cHeliosSizing
DocuSign template for enterprise terms: https://app.docusign.com/templates/Helios-Enterprise-Template

I'll attach the breakdown CSV to share with Elena.

Marcus
Capacity Planner, Redwood Inference
--Attachment: pool_split_breakdown.xlsx (spreadsheet)--

From: Elena Torres <elena@helios.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@helios.ai>, Ops Contact <ops@codalabs.ai>
Date: 2027-08-04T14:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Initial capacity ask — global footprint

Alex / team — thanks for the quick turnaround and the clarity. A few notes from procurement and ops: 

- Procurement prefers the Baseline plan for initial commit (US 36 / EU 24 / APAC 18) but asks if we can shift 6 APAC GPUs into an accelerated pilot window. In short: we need Singapore pilot capacity within 4 weeks for an EMEA+APAC launch readiness demo.
- Ops (Codalabs) requests that the APAC nodes be deployed in a private VPC with customer-managed KMS for the pilot.
- We are okay with 8-bit quantization on chat if Redwood certifies no material quality regression on our evaluation prompts.

Procurement timeline: internal PO approval expected by 08/12; final signature by 08/20. Can Redwood provide a firm APAC onboarding ETA and any one-time onboarding fees so we can include in the PO?

Best,
Elena
VP Product, Helios AI
--Attachment: apac_pilot_scope.docx (doc)--

2) Hannah: confirm partner availability in Singapore for private VPC + KMS workflow; list any gating security requirements and ETA delta for private VPC. Deliver by 2027-08-11 12:00 PT.

- We have these open windows (PDT):
  • Mon 19 Jul 10:00–11:30
  • Tue 20 Jul 16:00–17:30 (we can extend to 18:00 if needed)
  • Wed 21 Jul 18:30–20:00 (evening option)

Cc: sales-ops@redwood.com
Date: Tue, 29 Jun 2027 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Action: Orphaned opportunities cleanup + final commit window

Team —

Quick note ahead of week close: Sales Ops pulled a batch of ~48 opportunities that are currently \"Negotiation/Review\" but show no owner activity in the last 60 days and have missing/placeholder close dates. These are high noise for Finance forecast; we need to triage before the Friday commit window.

Ask: please review the attached CSV and do one of the following by 11:00 PT on Thursday (2027-06-30):
- Confirm commit: update SFDC stage to the correct stage and set a realistic close date
- No-Go / Archive: update stage to \"Not Pursuing\" and add reason (no budget / no timeline)
- Reassign: if you are not the right owner, reassign and add a one-line note in the Opportunity comments field

How I prioritized: batch contains deals > $25k ARR where Opportunity Age > 120 days and contact activity < 2 touches in 60 days. This is a lightweight hygiene pass — the goal is to remove noisy records from the forecast not to reopen cold pursuits.

If you need the list split or want a short triage slot, I have 15 minute blocks tomorrow (Wed) 10:15–11:00 and 14:00–15:00 PT. Reply with a slot and I'll send a calendar invite.

Attachment: 20270630-orphaned-opps-batch-1.csv (columns: OppId, Account, Owner, Stage, Amount, CloseDate, LastActivityDate, Notes)

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Senior Sales Operations Manager
Redwood Inference
ava_chen@redwood.com

title: Discrepancy: Invoice batch 042 deductions vs ledger - proposal to bridge
preview:
From: Emma Harris <emma.harris@novacloud.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Cc: Novacloud Billing <billing@novacloud.com>
Date: Mon, 21 Jun 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Discrepancy on batch 042: deductions applied but no credit memo posted

Hi Aditya,

Hope you’re well. We processed invoice batch 042 on 2027-05-30 and applied a standard 5% early-payment deduction to several line items. Our AR team shows the deduction was reflected on Novacloud's statement but we cannot find the corresponding credit memo or journal entry on your side to reconcile the unapplied balance.

Summary:
- Invoice batch: 042 (attached invoice_batch_042.pdf)
- Total billed: EUR 124,500
- Deductions applied by Novacloud AP: EUR 6,225 (5% on select invoices)
- Novacloud statement shows a net payment of EUR 118,275, but Redwood ledger still lists EUR 124,500 as open on several AR lines

Can you confirm whether a credit memo or AR adjustment was posted? If not, we need a journal correction or application guidance so AP can mark our account as cleared. Our month-end is approaching and we need this done by 2027-06-30.

Attaching our remittance advice and the excerpted statement. Happy to jump on a quick call if helpful.

Thanks,
Emma Harris
Senior AP Manager
Novacloud Ltd.

Attachments: invoice_batch_042.pdf (invoice list, 8 pages); novacloud_statement_may.pdf (statement excerpt)

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
To: Emma Harris <emma.harris@novacloud.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 21 Jun 2027 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Discrepancy on batch 042: deductions applied but no credit memo posted

Hi Emma — thanks for flagging and for the attachments. Sorry for the friction here. I’ve looped in Marcus (Accounting) and Laura (Revenue Ops) to confirm ledger postings and propose an action.

Quick plan:
1) Marcus will review AR ledger entries and post any necessary credit memos or journal corrections.
2) If Redwood posted net invoices incorrectly, we’ll issue a formal credit memo and notify ProcureDesk AP to reapply the remittance.
3) We’ll confirm expected posting date and update your statement in HubSpot.

Information we need from your side to speed this: the exact remittance file (CSV) used by your bank and the AP voucher IDs for the deductions. If you can share the remittance CSV, we’ll match on trace numbers.

Marcus / Laura — assigning this to you for investigation. Laura, please also check whether this affects revenue recognition mapping for May close (we have several customers on netting terms).

I’m available 2–4pm PT today if you want a quick sync.

Best,
Aditya Rao
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced: novacloud_statement_may.pdf (from Emma)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 22 Jun 2027 14:28:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Discrepancy on batch 042: deductions applied but no credit memo posted

Thanks for the ping — I dug into ledger. Findings below (summary):

Investigation notes:
- Posted invoices from batch 042 were entered gross (EUR 124,500) on 2027-05-30. No credit memos were created on Redwood side.
- Bank remittance shows net payment EUR 118,275. Remittance trace IDs present for invoices 042-1001 through 042-1008.
- Two invoices (042-1004 and 042-1007) had discount terms flagged; our ERP did not auto-generate credit memos because discounts were entered as negative receipts on the bank feed (they were posted to a suspense account 2405_suspense_ar).

Suggested remediation (low risk, reversible):
1) Post two credit memos for 042-1004 (EUR 1,950) and 042-1007 (EUR 4,275) matching the 5% deduction line items.
2) Move EUR 6,225 out of 2405_suspense_ar into AR offsets and apply to the open invoices.
3) Create a reversing journal for the suspense account dated 2027-06-25 with memo "Novacloud deduction reclass - batch 042".
4) Notify Novacloud AP and ProcureDesk to re-run cash application if needed.

Impact to revenue recognition: minimal — we'll book the credit memos as customer discounts and apply to the corresponding invoices. Laura, please confirm if any rev rec adjustments needed for May close; my read is this hits contra-revenue in Jun if we post dated 06/25.

I can draft the credit memos and the journal entries; need Aditya or Laura’s approval to post. If approved, I’ll schedule the posting for 2027-06-25 EOD and circulate the GL entries.

Entries I’ll prepare (preview):
- CM 042-CM-1004: Credit memo; EN: AR-042-1004; Amount EUR 1,950; Reason: early-payment deduction
- CM 042-CM-1007: Credit memo; EN: AR-042-1007; Amount EUR 4,275; Reason: early-payment deduction
- JE reversing suspense: DR 2405_suspense_ar EUR 6,225 / CR 1100_accounts_receivable EUR 6,225; Memo: Novacloud deduction reclass

Let me know — I’ll hold on posting until I get sign-off.

—Marcus
Marcus Lin
Senior Accounting Manager, Redwood Inference

Attachments: draft_cm_042.zip (contains CM PDFs and JE csv)

From: Emma Harris <emma.harris@novacloud.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Cc: ProcureDesk AP <ap@procuredesk.com>, Novacloud Billing <billing@novacloud.com>
Date: Fri, 25 Jun 2027 11:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Discrepancy on batch 042: deductions applied but no credit memo posted

Thanks Marcus — that plan works for us. We’ve attached the remittance file ProcureDesk used to post the payment (remittance_advice_0625.csv). ProcureDesk AP confirmed they will re-run the cash application once you publish the credit memos and notify them.

Action items agreed:
- Marcus to post CM 042-CM-1004 and 042-CM-1007 and reverse suspense JE on 2027-06-25.
- Aditya to confirm once posted so we can confirm with ProcureDesk AP.
- Laura to advise if any revenue recognition exceptions occur for May close (Novacloud is open to adjusting close timing if needed).

Please cc me once entries are live; we expect ProcureDesk to clear the net payment within 48 hours of your posting.

Appreciate the quick turnaround.

Best regards,
Emma Harris
Senior AP Manager
Novacloud Ltd.

Attachments: remittance_advice_0625.csv (bank-level trace IDs)

title: Urgent: WebSocket stalls & p99 spikes during peak for Clearline — exec alerted
preview:
From: Claire Morris <claire.morris@clearline.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Cc: Paul Nguyen <paul.nguyen@clearline.ai>
Date: Thu, 17 Jun 2027 16:12:00 -0400
Subject: Re: user-impacting stalls on hosted websocket chat during 17:00-18:30 PT

Aditya — we started seeing a sharp increase in stalls and connection resets on your hosted websocket endpoint during our evening peak (17:00-18:30 PT). p95 is up ~2.1s (ok) but p99 is hitting ~21-28s for several requests and many clients show repeated reconnects. Attached a screenshot from our in-app telemetry and a small packet dump. This began ~17:05 and has persisted in ~40% of sessions.

This is impacting live customer interactions — some messages take so long they time out on our side. Can you confirm whether anything changed on your side or if this is a capacity/backpressure issue? We need an ETA on mitigation.

Attachments: screenshot_peak_1715.png, websocket_dumps_0617.tar.gz

Claire Morris
Head of Product Ops — Clearline Health
claire.morris@clearline.ai


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 17 Jun 2027 16:25:00 -0400
Subject: Fwd: user-impacting stalls on hosted websocket chat during 17:00-18:30 PT

Team — forwarding Clearline's report. They see p99s in the 20s and mass reconnects during their evening peak starting ~17:05 PT. They've attached a packet dump and screenshot (included). They haven't changed client side in the last 72h.

Please triage immediately. Ask: 1) can we identify a model / node-level hotspot? 2) can we enable an automatic fallback to a lower-latency model or force shorter-context responses as a quick mitigation? 3) are there any throttling/backpressure events in the router?

I'll keep the customer updated; Monica pls prep CS note.

-Aditya

---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Claire Morris <claire.morris@clearline.ai>
Date: Thu, 17 Jun 2027 16:12:00 -0400
Subject: Re: user-impacting stalls on hosted websocket chat during 17:00-18:30 PT
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>

[original message and attachments]


From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 17 Jun 2027 16:48:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: user-impacting stalls on hosted websocket chat during 17:00-18:30 PT

Quick triage notes — I'm seeing the same p99 skew on our ingested traces in Datadog for the Clearline tenant. Initial observations:
- Around 17:03-17:07 PT there's a sharp uptick in long-tail token generation stalls tied to a subset of websocket connections that send bursty multi-chunk messages.
- Runtime traces show repeated KV cache misses and a rise in repeated model reloads on a small set of nodes (likely due to our eviction heuristics interacting poorly with their session patterns).
- No recent kernel or infra deploys in the region at that time; however, a config tweak to the eviction TTL rolled out yesterday may have increased cache churn under peak concurrency.

Immediate actions I'm proposing (can implement in next 30m):
1) Pin Clearline traffic to a warm pool (evict-safe) to avoid reloads — reduces cold starts but consumes reserved capacity.
2) Temporarily enable model-backoff/faster truncation for websocket sessions from this tenant to reduce per-request tokens (non-invasive fallback).
3) Increase router queue depth and apply stronger backpressure at the websocket ingress to prevent downstream overload.

Also requesting Clearline share a few sample session IDs (we have one from their dump) so we can reproduce locally.

I'll push a Datadog snapshot and a perf dump to the incident ticket.

-Dev


From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 17 Jun 2027 17:10:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: user-impacting stalls on hosted websocket chat during 17:00-18:30 PT

I created an isolated warm pool and pinned Clearline for a failover test; also rolled back the TTL tweak on that pool. Early metrics (5m) show their p99 dropping from ~24s to ~3.7s for pinned traffic. Attached a short perf snapshot (dd_perf_snapshot.png).

I'm going to enable model-backoff (option 2 from Dev) for websocket sessions originating from their app key and toggle a more aggressive gating rule on the websocket ingress to shed load gracefully. These are non-disruptive to connections but will reduce token consumption.

If customer approves, we should schedule a 30m maintenance window to shift their production traffic to the pinned pool so we get stable behavior while we refine eviction heuristics.

-Evelyn

Attachment: dd_perf_snapshot.png


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
To: Claire Morris <claire.morris@clearline.ai>, Paul Nguyen <paul.nguyen@clearline.ai>, Clearline Ops <ops@clearline.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 17 Jun 2027 17:33:00 -04:00
Subject: RE: user-impacting stalls on hosted websocket chat during 17:00-18:30 PT

Claire — thanks for the quick report and for sending the dump. Engineering has identified cache churn caused by a recent TTL change as the likely trigger. We've spun up an isolated warm pool and enabled model-backoff for your websocket sessions; initial perf indicates p99 improvement.

Proposed next step: with your approval we'd like to perform a 30-minute maintenance window tomorrow at 17:00 PT to route your traffic to the pinned pool and observe behavior under peak. There will be no planned downtime — this is a controlled routing change to stabilize tail latency. We'll provide a post-window summary and escalate for SLA compensation if we confirm an impact on your side.

Monica from CS will coordinate customer-facing comms. Please confirm if tomorrow 17:00 PT works or propose alternatives.

-Aditya


From: Tom Baker <tom.baker@clearline.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Cc: Claire Morris <claire.morris@clearline.ai>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 19 Jun 2027 17:45:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: user-impacting stalls on hosted websocket chat during 17:00-18:30 PT

Aditya / Monica — this is Tom (CTO). Our exec team is closely watching this — we had several large customers complain yesterday evening and our ops team recorded multiple session timeouts. We appreciate the mitigation so far but request an executive briefing (30m) to review root cause, customer impact, and a timeline for a durable fix to eviction heuristics.

Please propose two time slots Monday (06/21): 10:00 PT or 15:00 PT. We'd like Redwood engineering and product leadership on the call. Also include any interim mitigations and an estimate of potential SLA credit exposure.

Tom Baker

- Runtime traces show repeated KV cache misses and a rise in repeated model reloads on a small set of nodes (likely due to our eviction heuristics interacting poorly with their session patterns).
- No recent kernel or infra deploys in the region at that time; however, a config tweak to the eviction TTL rolled out yesterday may have increased cache churn under peak concurrency.

Immediate actions I'm proposing (can implement in next 30m):
1) Pin Clearline traffic to a warm pool (evict-safe) to avoid reloads — reduces cold starts but consumes reserved capacity.
2) Temporarily enable model-backoff/faster truncation for websocket sessions from this tenant to reduce per-request tokens (non-invasive fallback).
3) Increase router queue depth and apply stronger backpressure at the websocket ingress to prevent downstream overload.

Also requesting Clearline share a few sample session IDs (we have one from their dump) so we can reproduce locally.

I'll push a Datadog snapshot and a perf dump to the incident ticket.

-Dev


From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 17 Jun 2027 17:10:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: user-impacting stalls on hosted websocket chat during 17:00-18:30 PT

I created an isolated warm pool and pinned Clearline for a failover test; also rolled back the TTL tweak on that pool. Early metrics (5m) show their p99 dropping from ~24s to ~3.7s for pinned traffic. Attached a short perf snapshot (dd_perf_snapshot.png).

I'm going to enable model-backoff (option 2 from Dev) for websocket sessions originating from their app key and toggle a more aggressive gating rule on the websocket ingress to shed load gracefully. These are non-disruptive to connections but will reduce token consumption.

If customer approves, we should schedule a 30m maintenance window to shift their production traffic to the pinned pool so we get stable behavior while we refine eviction heuristics.

-Evelyn

Attachment: dd_perf_snapshot.png


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
To: Claire Morris <claire.morris@clearline.ai>, Paul Nguyen <paul.nguyen@clearline.ai>, Clearline Ops <ops@clearline.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 17 Jun 2027 17:33:00 -04:00
Subject: RE: user-impacting stalls on hosted websocket chat during 17:00-18:30 PT

Claire — thanks for the quick report and for sending the dump. Engineering has identified cache churn caused by a recent TTL change as the likely trigger. We've spun up an isolated warm pool and enabled model-backoff for your websocket sessions; initial perf indicates p99 improvement.

Proposed next step: with your approval we'd like to perform a 30-minute maintenance window tomorrow at 17:00 PT to route your traffic to the pinned pool and observe behavior under peak. There will be no planned downtime — this is a controlled routing change to stabilize tail latency. We'll provide a post-window summary and escalate for SLA compensation if we confirm an impact on your side.

Monica from CS will coordinate customer-facing comms. Please confirm if tomorrow 17:00 PT works or propose alternatives.

-Aditya


From: Tom Baker <tom.baker@clearline.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Cc: Claire Morris <claire.morris@clearline.ai>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 19 Jun 2027 17:45:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: user-impacting stalls on hosted websocket chat during 17:00-18:30 PT

Aditya / Monica — this is Tom (CTO). Our exec team is closely watching this — we had several large customers complain yesterday evening and our ops team recorded multiple session timeouts. We appreciate the mitigation so far but request an executive briefing (30m) to review root cause, customer impact, and a timeline for a durable fix to eviction heuristics.

Please propose two time slots Monday (06/21): 10:00 PT or 15:00 PT. We'd like Redwood engineering and product leadership on the call. Also include any interim mitigations and an estimate of potential SLA credit exposure.

Tom Baker

- Wed Jun 23: 14:00–17:00
- Thu Jun 24: 09:00–12:00 (alternate)

- Aditya

Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

title: Deadline breach: cordon & recovery plan for ACME evaluation
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Priya Singh <priya.singh@acme.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 7 Jun 2027 09:15:00 -0700
Subject: Deadline breach: cordon & recovery plan for ACME evaluation

Hi Priya —

Quick update from last night's acceptance run: the final eval set hit a regression in multi-turn context handling that causes the acceptance criteria to fail intermittently. We ran a focused repro overnight and confirmed the root cause is related to a tokenizer edge-case when system prompts contain the customer metadata header.

Given that this directly impacts the close timeline, we recommend an immediate cordon (freeze) on any non-critical changes into the eval path and the following recovery steps:

- Cordon window: effective now until we confirm a stable acceptance run (expected 48–72 hours)
- Triage & owner: Jonas to drive triage with the SRE/infra patch; Grace to coordinate test permutations and acceptance harness updates
- Daily sync: 10:00 PT standup (15m) with customer leads and our engineers until green
- MAP update: propose moving Acceptance Complete milestone +10 business days, provisional — we need your buy-in to lock the calendar
- Escalation: if we don't reach stable pass within 72 hours, we will escalate to your procurement lead for a formal exception request and to our sales leader for credit/SLAs

I attached the acceptance-fail-log.csv and a short recovery-plan-v1.pdf with the proposed triage timeline. Can you confirm availability for the daily 10:00 PT sync and whether Tom should be looped in on procurement-side contingency?

Apologies for the late notice — want to own this and get us back to the target close without surprise.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Principal Customer Success Manager
Redwood Inference

Attachments: acceptance-fail-log.csv (text/csv), recovery-plan-v1.pdf (application/pdf)

From: Priya Singh <priya.singh@acme.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@acme.com>
Date: Mon, 7 Jun 2027 11:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Deadline breach: cordon & recovery plan for ACME evaluation

Ava —

Thanks for the quick heads up and for owning the recovery. This is concerning given our internal go-live commitments. I can join the 10:00 PT standups. I would like a short write-up on the exact acceptance test that failed (input + expected/actual) so our QA can review in parallel.

Tom: looping you in to confirm whether the +10 business day provisional milestone is acceptable from ACME procurement's perspective, or if we should start formalizing a timeline exception.

Priya
Director, Product Operations
ACME Corporation

On Mon, Jun 7, 2027 at 09:15 Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com> wrote:
> [quoted message omitted]


From: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
To: Priya Singh <priya.singh@acme.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 8 Jun 2027 08:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Deadline breach: cordon & recovery plan for ACME evaluation

Priya / Ava —

Short technical update and the concrete asks from engineering:

1) Repro & fix ETA
- We reproduced the tokenizer edge-case locally; root cause is a rare token-split on injected metadata (happens ~0.6% of runs with current eval set)
- Fix: small tokenizer normalization + a hotfix to the eval harness to strip/escape the problematic header — we expect a build and can run a staged acceptance by tomorrow EOD (Wed 2027-06-09) if we get approval to proceed

2) Required access / approvals
- Need permission to run a limited canary in your staging environment (read-only logs only).
- If acceptable, please confirm staging hostname and the service account we should use; Grace will coordinate test harness changes

3) MAP and timeline recommendation
- Proposed MAP change: Acceptance Complete -> 2027-06-22 (previously 2027-06-10) — this is conservative to give buffer for intermittent flakiness and a full regression pass
- We will publish an updated MAP spreadsheet after the first green run; Marcus will attach the updated calendar

4) Communication cadence
- Daily 10:00 PT sync (15m) — quick blocker list + owner/ETA
- Status note to be sent to Tom and your execs at 18:00 PT daily until fixed

Ava — please confirm the canary in their staging and coordinate the 10:00 standups. Priya / Tom — please confirm if the +12 business day rebaseline (to 2027-06-22) is acceptable; if procurement needs a formal exception doc, we can push that by EOD today.

Thanks,
Jonas Weber
Director, Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference

> On Mon, Jun 7, 2027 at 11:30 Priya Singh <priya.singh@acme.com> wrote:
> > [quoted message omitted]


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Priya Singh <priya.singh@acme.com>, Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@acme.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Jun 2027 16:40:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Deadline breach: cordon & recovery plan for ACME evaluation

Priya / Tom —

Following the successful staged acceptance this morning (we saw the pass rate climb from 99.4% to 99.98% on the canonical eval), we've attached an updated MAP calendar (updated-map-20270610.xlsx) and a slightly tightened recovery runbook. Key points:

- New acceptance milestone: 2027-06-21 (we trimmed one day after the green run, still includes buffer)
- Root cause fix: deployed to staging and validated; fix will be in the next PR to prod gate (scheduled Saturday maintenance)
- Credits/SLAs: we will hold off on formal credit conversations unless a further regression occurs; we documented the contingency for procurement in the attachment

Next steps for ACME:
- Please confirm acceptance of the 2027-06-21 milestone by EOD tomorrow so we can update internal calendars and Ops-on-call rota
- If confirmation is not received by EOD, per our escalation policy we will escalate to my manager and your procurement lead for an exception/credit decision

I appreciate the patience — team did a focused job to limit the slip and keep the MAP changes minimal. Drive-by notes: Grace will publish the test permutations and the acceptance harness diffs to the Drive link in the related_links field.

Marcus Lin
Senior Program Manager, Customer Success
Redwood Inference

Attachments: updated-map-20270610.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet)

title: Deployment pathway scenarios & sizing proforma
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Elena Park <elena@novapay.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-10T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Post-call recap + proforma request (hosted vs dedicated vs private)

Hi Elena — thanks again for the call this morning. Quick recap and next steps so we're aligned on sizing and deployment pathway options: 

1) What we heard from NovaPay:
- Primary use: tokenization + conversational support inside your merchant dashboard for fraud triage and dispute summaries.
- Peak TPS target: ~250 short-completion calls per second during batch settlement windows (peaks last ~10–15 min). Off-peak ~30 TPS.
- Token mix: average request ~220 input tokens, 140 output tokens; 95thpct requests up to 1,200 tokens (long receipts + context).
- Latency target: p95 < 350ms for short completions, p95 < 1.2s for long-context ops.
- Data residency: US-only; SOC2 evidence required.

2) Deployment pathways we discussed:
- Hosted API: fastest to production but shared pool; recommend only for non-sensitive early rollout.
- Dedicated capacity: reserved GPU pools (best predictability for your TPS bursts), supports KV/prefix caching to lower cost on repeated contexts.
- Private VPC/on-prem: for eventual regulated workflows; longer lead time and integration work.

3) Sizing asks we need from you to produce a proforma (we added a basic worksheet attached):
- Clarify percent of calls that are short (<400 tokens) vs long (>400 tokens) during peak.
- Any expected concurrency from your frontend (concurrent open sessions interacting with chat/generate API)?
- Confirmation if 99% of requests will be within US region.

Next steps: if you can confirm the short/long mix and typical concurrency by Friday, we'll return a dedicated-capacity proforma (GPU mix options + cost/latency tradeoffs) and a hosted fall-back plan.

Attachments: I attached the blank proforma and our SOC2 summary for procurement.

Cheers,
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

--
Ava Chen
Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.com
(415) 555-0192


From: Elena Park <elena@novapay.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-10T10:07:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + proforma request (hosted vs dedicated vs private)

Hi Ava — thanks for the tidy recap. Confirming the numbers from our end: 

- Peak TPS window: 250 TPS (as discussed). We expect this to be concentrated in three 12–15 minute windows per day.
- Token mix estimate (rough): ~65% short (<=400 tokens), ~30% medium (401–800), ~5% long (>800).
- Average concurrency: frontend keeps ~1.8k concurrent open sessions during peak; estimate ~15% of those actively sending requests within any 30s window. That yields roughly 270 concurrent active calls during peaks (customer-side estimate).
- Residency: US-only. SOC2 path okay; will request SOC2 + encryption attestation from procurement.

I've uploaded our short transcript logs to the Drive folder you shared; feel free to use those for more accurate token profiling.

Elena
Director, Engineering Ops, NovaPay
ops@novapay.com


From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-10T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: sizing numbers from NovaPay — asking for GPU recommendations and proforma inputs

Rafael / Marcus — forwarding Elena's confirmation above. Key constraints: 250 TPS peak, token mix ~65/30/5, ~270 concurrent active calls in peak window, p95 latency goals. Can you propose: 

- 2–3 dedicated GPU SKU options (e.g., H100-heavy vs A100 mixed vs L4 for short inference) with expected token/s GPU TPS and expected p95 latency for short vs long requests. Please include KV cache sizing assumptions and whether multi-node sharding or model variants (quantized) are recommended.
- Recommended batching/concurrency (tokens-in-flight) defaults we would set in the proforma to meet p95 SLA targets while minimizing cost.
- Any redlines on feasibility for 1.2s for long-context ops at 250 TPS peak.

I'll combine your inputs into the attached proforma and send back to Elena with cost/latency tradeoffs and timeline for Dedicated vs Hosted pilot.

Thanks,
Ava


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-10T13:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: sizing numbers from NovaPay — GPU recommendations

Quick pass from infra: high level guidance below; we can turn these into the proforma numbers once Marcus confirms cost model tweaks.

Option A — H100-focused (lowest latency for long context):
- Single H100 (80GB) perf note: ~8–11 short-completion TPS at 1/seq=140/resp=140 with aggressive kernel selection; with batching/prefetch we can push effective short-completion throughput closer to 18–20 TPS per GPU for highly quantized 8-bit models.
- For NovaPay peak (250 TPS short-heavy), estimate 12–16 H100s if we run mixed batching and reserve headroom for KV cache hot keys. Long requests (1,200 tokens) gravitate to 2–3x higher GPU time; plan for additional concurrency headroom (estimate +30% GPUs to keep p95 under 1.2s).
- KV/cache: dedicating ~200GB per pool for prefix/KV cache recommended (fast SSD-backed local cache across nodes).

Option B — A100-mix (cost / latency balance):
- A100 40GB: ~6–9 effective TPS for medium short requests, but denser packing and cheaper per-hour. For your mix, ~20 A100s gives predictable behavior and lower $/token than H100 option but slightly higher p95 on long runs.

Option C — Hybrid with L4 for short completions + H100 for long context:
- Offload >60% of short requests to L4 instances (cheaper), and reserve H100s for long-context and fallback. This reduces cost significantly but adds routing complexity and model-variant validation.

Batching/course defaults: 12–20ms target batching window for short completions; limit dynamic-batch size to keep p95 < 350ms. Recommend adaptive batching that tightens when queue latency approaches threshold.

Feasibility note: hitting 1.2s p95 for 1,200-token requests at 250 TPS is possible but expensive if all requests are long — our sizing assumes only ~5% long requests as you reported. If long-request % increases, GPU counts scale superlinearly.

I'll prepare precise GPU counts and cost delta for A/B variants (H100-only, A100 mix, hybrid) and drop into the proforma.

- Rafael
Senior Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-10T15:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: sizing numbers from NovaPay — GPU recommendations

Adding pricing and procurement timeline notes for the proforma: 

- Ballpark monthly committed pricing (pre-negotiation): H100 cluster (16 nodes) estimated $XX,XXX/mo; A100 cluster (20 nodes) estimated $YY,YYY/mo; Hybrid (L4 + H100 reserve) ~midpoint. I'll populate exact $/token and committed vs on-demand deltas in the spreadsheet.
- Lead times: Dedicated pool provisioning (US region) ~4–6 weeks once contract signed (shorter if we can use reserved infra we have queued). Private VPC onboarding ~8–12 weeks (adds network review + KMS integration).
- Procurement ask: include SOC2 docs and an initial PO estimate to unblock infra procurement.

I'll update nova_capacity_proforma.xlsx with node counts, expected headroom, and per-node throughput assumptions and send a versioned copy back to you. Ava — once you get Rafael's final numbers, please attach the proforma and a DoR checklist for NovaPay procurement; we should ask for a soft commit on timeline in the reply.

Marcus
Sales Engineering Manager, Redwood Inference
