title: Cross-cloud evaluation playbook + next steps (post-demo)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Samantha Price <samantha.price@cloudforge.com>
Date: Mon, 7 Jun 2027 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Cross-cloud evaluation playbook + next steps (post-demo)

Team —

Quick recap from this morning's call with CloudForge (Samantha) and a tentative action plan. High-level takeaways:

- CloudForge is running initial comparisons against two hyperscaler inference endpoints (HyperscaleA, HyperEdge) and saw p95 deltas that worry their infra team for real-time scoring (p95 ~210–290ms on HyperEdge vs ~110–140ms on Redwood-hosted variant in our demo). They want a reproducible A/B run.
- Primary ask: a concise A/B protocol they can run (or we run jointly) to measure p95/p99 and sustained tokens/sec at 256/1024/2048 sequence lengths.
- Secondary ask: cost-per-million-tokens estimate across the configurations and a short write-up on fallbacks (model variants + quantization) that preserve latency.

Proposed next steps (please action):

1) Benchmark playbook draft (Marcus): produce a short YAML/config that codifies runtime variants, concurrency, payload shapes, and success criteria (p95 < 200ms for interactive use; p99 < 350ms). Include cold-start and steady-state runs.

2) Test matrix (Jordan): pick the 3 windows for a joint run with CloudForge next week — we should offer at least two time slots in their PDT window. Include a canary run that uses our quantized-8bit variant and a baseline float16 run.

3) Data & artifacts (Ben): share the call recording and initial CSV they sent, plus a HubSpot link & folder for artifacts. I'll coordinate logistics with Arjun at Edgecraft if we need alternate infra.

Bench metrics to capture (quick reference):
- p50 / p95 / p99 latency (server-side)
- tokens/sec (steady-state with concurrency sweep)
- end-to-end wall time (network + server)
- cost estimate: CPU/GPU time translated to token unit economics

Attachments: CF-call-recording.mp4 (recording), cloudforge-initial-metrics.csv (their quick probe).

Samantha — thanks again for the time. Jordan will send calendar options; Marcus will post a config by EOD tomorrow. If there are additional measurements your SRE wants, drop them in the thread and we'll fold them into the playbook.

Ben
Ben Carter | Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0123
https://redwood.ai

---

[Attachment: CF-call-recording.mp4, cloudforge-initial-metrics.csv]

Cc: Samantha Price <samantha.price@cloudforge.com>
Date: Mon, 7 Jun 2027 12:10:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Cross-cloud evaluation playbook + next steps (post-demo)

Ben — thanks for the recap. A couple of clarifications from my side:

- Success criteria: let's be explicit in the playbook about canary thresholds vs production SLOs. Canary pass = p95 within 1.5x of our demo baseline and p99 within 2x; production target = p95 < 200ms.
- Add a quantized-8bit (Q8) variant as an explicit test row — many prospects see big perf gains there with small quality regressions.
- Offer these time slots to CloudForge for next Wed/Thu: 10:30–12:00 PDT or 14:00–16:00 PDT (we should reserve infra for both).

I'll take point on calendar coordination and can jump into the run to handle routing/fallback configs. Marcus — can you include the exact artifact names you need (bench-config.yml, sample payloads, and a small harness script)?

Jordan
Jordan Blake | Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
jordan.blake@redwood.ai

> > Quick recap from this morning's call...


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>

Cc: Samantha Price <samantha.price@cloudforge.com>, Arjun Patel <arjun@edgecraft.ai>
Date: Tue, 8 Jun 2027 09:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Cross-cloud evaluation playbook + next steps (post-demo)

Ben / Jordan —

I'll draft the bench-config.yml and run an initial sweep on our staging Dedicated pool tomorrow morning. Notes / engineering asks up front:

- Please confirm payload shapes: sampling vs streaming; we should include both (streaming token flush affects measured p95).
- We'll measure cold-start (first request after idle) and steady-state (after KV cache warmed; target window: 2000 requests).
- Concurrency sweep: [1, 4, 8, 16, 32] across seq lengths [256, 1024, 2048].
- Model variants: baseline (float16), quantized-8bit (Q8), and smaller distilled variant (if CloudForge is open to a quality check).
- Metrics: p50/p95/p99 (server), tokens/sec (server measured), payload RTT (client-observed). We'll emit CSVs with run labels and seed.

I'll attach the initial bench-config.yml skeleton and a short run script stub. I'm leaning toward measuring tokens/sec on a sustained 60s window per concurrency point to avoid sampling noise. Also — recommend we run the in-region test (us-west) to avoid cross-region network noise.

Marcus
Marcus Lin | Infrastructure & Performance Engineer, Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.ai

Date: Tue, 8 Jun 2027 11:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Cross-cloud evaluation playbook + next steps (post-demo)

Ben / Jordan / Marcus —

Thanks — this looks great and very actionable. A few quick replies from CloudForge SRE side:

- Payloads: we primarily use streaming scoring for short-form inputs (~150 tokens) and full-sequence scoring for larger documents (up to 2k). We'll share sample payloads by EOD.
- We agree on the concurrency sweep. Please include a client-side synthetic that reproduces our traffic pattern (bursty microbursts).
- We do need a signed mutual NDA addendum to share some internal tokenized datasets for quality checks. Can someone loop Legal?

Happy to confirm the Wed/Thu slots; 10:30–12:00 PDT on Wed works best. Please send a calendar invite with dial-in + shared drive folder.

Samantha Price
Director, Platform Engineering — CloudForge
samantha.price@cloudforge.com

Date: Wed, 9 Jun 2027 09:12:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Cross-cloud evaluation playbook + next steps (post-demo)

All —

Great. Quick logistics & owner recap before the Wed run (10:30–12:00 PDT):

- Marcus: bench-config.yml + run script (deliverable: bench-config.yml, run-output-raw.csv) — owner for test execution.
- Jordan: calendar invite, infra reservations, and live routing checks (canary rules) — owner for run ops.
- Ben: NDA follow-up with Legal and artifact collation + cost estimates after runs.

Please upload artifacts to the shared folder: https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/folders/CF-CROSSCLOUD-BENCH (I will seed it with the recording + their CSV).

Success criteria (final):
- Canary acceptable if p95 <= 1.5x our demo baseline AND tokens/sec >= 70% of our steady-state baseline at matching concurrency.
- If quantized-8bit passes canary with QoE < 3% delta vs baseline, propose it as a cost-saving fallback.

I'll ping Legal on the NDA today and send the invite in the hour. Thanks everyone — see you on Wed.

Ben

Subject: Request: cohort top-up proration & partner credit

Hi Ben —

Quick ask from our side as the current cohort nears week 5: usage spiked (attaching snapshot) and we’re about to blow through the free cohort allowance earlier than expected. Two asks: 1) can Redwood prorate/bridge a one-time top-up of credits for this cohort to cover the overage through end of the cohort (roughly +$6k in spend), and 2) is there any partner credit you can apply (we co-run the program with LaunchFoundry and they typically get a small partner offset)?

We’re finalizing a follow-up SOW for the next cohort and would commit to a 6-month consumption minimum if we get a one-time accommodation here. Attaching usage snapshot + SOW draft. Happy to jump on a quick call today if helpful.

Thanks —
Ethan
Ops, Catalyst Labs
Attachments: cohort-usage-snapshot-May2027.csv (CSV), SOW-draft-v2.pdf (PDF)

Date: Sun, 23 May 2027 10:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: cohort top-up proration & partner credit

Hi Ethan —

Thanks for the heads up and for the context. I pulled your usage and see the spike tied to overnight ingest jobs the cohort ran. Two quick notes: we typically handle small overages via credits tied to the program, and partner offsets are possible but handled as a separate partner-credit ledger.

I'll escalate internally for a one-time top-up to cover ~6k of the overage through the end of the cohort. Can you confirm the cohort end date (you have it as 2027-06-07 in the SOW draft) and whether the 6k is net of any credit card/transaction fees?

Marissa / Naomi / Marcus —

Looping you in: Catalyst Labs (external) is asking for a one-time +$6k top-up to bridge an overage through 2027-06-07. They’re willing to commit to a 6-month minimum for the next cohort if we accommodate this.

What I’m proposing:
- Approve a one-time consumption credit of $4k from our program pool (accelerator allowance)
- Allow a partner-managed offset up to $2k if Marcus confirms partner ledger coverage (partner credit, not Redwood cash)
- Require the 6-month minimum on the follow-on SOW and a signed amendment to lock it in

This would keep the net Redwood impact at $4k while protecting us via contract. Any objections? If approved I’ll confirm to Ethan and send a DocuSign amendment template.

Cheers,
Ben

I’m okay to approve the $4k one-time program credit on two conditions:

Naomi

title: Next steps: vendor enrollment & tax docs after demo with StellarHealth
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Erin Shaw <erin.shaw@stellarhealth.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 24 May 2027 14:12:00 -0700
Subject: Next steps: vendor enrollment & tax docs after demo with StellarHealth

Hi Erin —

Thanks again for taking the time today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so procurement has everything they need to evaluate Redwood for production: 

1) Requested docs: W-9 (for US entity) or W-8BEN-E (if relevant) + completed vendor packet (banking info + ACH instructions).
2) Payment preference: you mentioned PO-first for initial pilot; please confirm whether Procurement wants invoices to 30 net and if they require a PO number to be present on invoices/contract.
3) Marketplace: you asked whether we can be transacted via the Cloud Marketplace (GCP/AWS). We support marketplace billing but it does add time for vendor enrollment — it can be a parallel path.
4) Vendor portal: if Procurement prefers a portal onboarding flow, we can send a vendor packet PDF and an onboarding link to complete tax and banking details.

I attached our W-9 and a short vendor packet with instructions. Can you confirm: PO vs card (which would speed pilot), and whether Stellar prefers a vendor portal enrollment or email-based paper process?

Proposed internal owners: Marcus (finance) will handle banking and tax details; Neha (onboarding) will manage portal/invite and the vendor packet.

If helpful, happy to jump on a quick 20-min call with Procurement later this week.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com | (415) 555-0192

Attachments: Redwood_W9_2027.pdf (application/pdf), vendor_packet_instructions_Stellar.pdf (application/pdf)

Thanks Ben — great demo this afternoon.

Short answers:
- We will almost certainly need a PO for the pilot (internal policy), so Procurement wants the PO route. Net30 is our standard payment term but I will confirm if pilot exceptions are allowed.
- Please send the vendor packet and W-9; I will need a completed packet back with banking details and a point of contact in case of questions.
- Marketplace would be nice, but procurement prioritizes getting the vendor set up and the PO issued first. We can revisit marketplace later.

Can you confirm whether Redwood requires a signed MSA before PO issuance or if we can do a PO with terms referenced and finalize the MSA in parallel? Also include a contact for Treasury so I can push the packet through.

Also, please generate a one-page list of expected timeline steps for Procurement (e.g., packet complete -> vendor ID -> PO issuance -> kickoff) that I can share with Erin.

I attached the W-9 again in case they missed it.

Date: Wed, 26 May 2027 10:15:00 -07:00

Hi all — quick finance confirmation: 

- The W-9 we provided (Redwood_W9_2027.pdf) is sufficient for US tax ID verification. For bank wiring/ACH we need the completed banking section of the vendor packet (account name, routing, account number) and a bank letter or voided check would expedite validation.
- We do not strictly require a signed MSA before a PO is issued; many customers issue a PO referencing agreed commercial terms and finalize the MSA in parallel. If Stellar needs redlines, send them over and we will prioritize review.
- For Treasury contact: please have them reach out to treasury@redwoodinference.com and reference DEAL-8342 to speed routing.

Marcus Lin

title: Security evidence mapping + calendar ops for SOC2/DPA review
Assimilation bundle: partner contracting & escalation matrix for vector stores
preview:
From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha@pinecone.io>, Alex DeVries <alex@weaviate.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 18 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Assimilation bundle: partner contracting & escalation matrix for vector stores

Hi Samantha / Alex —

Thanks again for the productive sync earlier. We walked away with a clear set of asks from both sides: Pinecone wants a concise SOW that maps operational responsibilities; Weaviate asked for clarity on the fallback routing and data residency clauses; and both teams requested an escalation matrix so field ops know who to loop in when index inconsistencies or cross-store failovers happen.

To move this to contracting + ops alignment, I've attached a proposed \"assimilation matrix\" (Redwood_Assimilation_Matrix_v1.xlsx) and a short SOW stub for Pinecone. Ask to the group: can you each review and call out non-starters by Friday?

Specifically, Redwood will own: model-to-index mapping config, SDK compatibility tests, and prefix/KV cache invalidation coordination. Partner asks to own: index failover SLA, cold-tier pull performance guarantees, and a documented debug runbook.

Next steps I propose:
- Internal review (Ben/Marcus/Laura): confirm Redwood responsibilities and owner names in the matrix by Wed EOD.
- Partner review (Samantha/Alex): mark redlines and operational gaps in the doc by Fri.
- If redlines exceed 3 substantive items, we schedule a contracts touchpoint next week.

Attachments: Redwood_Assimilation_Matrix_v1.xlsx (ops/roles), Initial_SOW_Pinecone_draft.pdf (commercial + SLAs), Ops_Escalation_Roles.docx (template)

Thanks —
Vanessa Ortiz
Head of Partnerships, Redwood Inference
vanessa_ortiz@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0148

---
(Internal note: I want to keep both Pinecone & Weaviate in scope but need concrete SLA language on cold-tiering and signing authority for runbook updates.)

From: Samantha Lee <samantha@pinecone.io>
To: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 19 May 2027 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Assimilation bundle: partner contracting & escalation matrix for vector stores

Vanessa —

Appreciate the clear ask and the docs. Quick read-through from our side (Pinecone):

- SOW: Our legal has a few standard clauses they will redline (liability cap tied to usage, data residency addendum for EU customers). We'll circle back with exact language.
- Ops SLA: We can commit to a 99.9% index availability for hot indexes and provide target 95th-percentile fetch latency for hot queries. Cold-tier pull performance is trickier; we propose a \"best-effort + throughput budget\" model rather than hard p99 SLA for cold pulls.
- Escalation matrix: happy to populate names for Tier 1/2 on our side; we will need Redwood's on-call rotation for crosswalk.

Quick questions:
1) Is Redwood planning to run the prefix/KV cache on Hosted or Dedicated? That affects where we expect cache invalidations to originate.
2) For the SOW, who at Redwood signs with partner vendors — legal or partnerships? We need a signing authority listed to accelerate DocuSign routing.

I'll push a marked-up SOW and an ops-sheet by Friday. In parallel I'll loop our contracts lead (cc'd offline) if we hit a sticking point.

Best,
Samantha Lee
Head of Strategic Alliances, Pinecone
samantha@pinecone.io

---
> On Tue, May 18, 2027 at 9:12 AM Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.ai> wrote:
> > Thanks again for the productive sync earlier....

(attachment: Pinecone_SOW_comments_placeholder.txt)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 20 May 2027 08:46:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Assimilation bundle: partner contracting & escalation matrix for vector stores

Vanessa — quick internal alignment.

High-level comments after my pass on the matrix and SOW:

Date: Fri, 21 May 2027 08:42:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Evidence request — security architecture diagrams + isolation model for Dedicated/Private (SIG & SOC2)

Hi all — attaching the first-pass artifacts for intake (see attachment stubs below). Quick notes inline.

Attachment stubs included:
- redwood_control_plane_fencing_v1.pdf (diagram, 3 pages) — shows control plane endpoints on a separate management VPC, cross-region routing overlay, and the automatic fallback route to pooled Dedicated capacity. Annotations include: KMS boundary (customer-managed keys for Private), control-plane API endpoints (mutually TLS authenticated), and control-plane DNS isolation.
- vpc_enclave_connectivity_diagram.png — shows per-tenant enclave placement, ingress points, prefix/KV cache boundaries, and egress NAT paths to customer-configured peering or transit gateway.
- segregation_matrix_table.xlsx — row per tenant class (public/self-service, enterprise-shared, enterprise-dedicated) with columns for routing zone, L4/L7 constraints, logging retention, and required attestations.

Narrative excerpt (pasted):
"Redwood enforces separation between control and data planes by placing orchestration/control services into a dedicated management VPC with no direct tenant data-plane egress. For Dedicated and Private deployments, tenant inference instances run in isolated enclaves with strict egress rules: either customer-managed peer links (Private) or our dedicated egress pools (Dedicated) governed by policy. Keys for customer data are stored in KMS instances scoped per-customer for Private deployments; for Dedicated we offer optional CMEK with HSM-backed KMS integration. Audit logs for control-plane operations are retained in an immutable logging bucket with retention policy configurable per contract."

A few action items:
- Elena: Do you want packet-filter examples or are the annotated ACL callouts sufficient?
- Samir: If you can confirm the list of reviewers who need shareable copies, we’ll prepare redacted and full versions.

We’ll hand off final PDF + spreadsheet by EOD today once we confirm redaction needs.

Thanks,
Kimberly Park
Principal Security Architect, Redwood Inference
kimberly_park@redwood.example

Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Lukas Meyer <lukas.meyer@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 20 May 2027 08:47:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Control-plane handshake & residency evidence for EU/APAC private topologies

Thanks Naomi — quick update from infra/arch side.

I attached our proposed VPC topology for review (vpc-topology-eu-apac.pdf). Key points: 
- Primary data plane: EU region (single primary cluster in eu-west-1 equivalent). All customer datasets are pinned to that cluster.
- Warm-standby in APAC: compute-only nodes in ap-northeast-1 with encrypted KV cache that contains only stateless runtime caches. No persisted customer data will be written to APAC disks.
- Failover handshake: control-plane uses mutual TLS with cert pinning; failover is initiated by health checks + control-plane quorum. A preconfigured routing policy will enforce that EU-origin requests (identified by headers) always prefer EU endpoints; APAC-origin requests prefer APAC.
- KMS: we propose customer-managed keys in EU KMS for data-at-rest. For control-plane certificates and ephemeral keys, we recommend a centralized HSM in EU with cross-region read-only replication for emergency unseal (audit-controlled).

Open questions for Anna/Paul:
1) Would EuCorp accept read-only DR unlock keys replicated to APAC under strict audit and with immediate revocation capability?
2) Are there vendors (cloud or colo) you cannot accept for peering?

I'll follow up with a CSV that maps endpoints to routing policies and expected RTO/RPO.

-Rafael

Anna — quick operational note on the handshake and failover matrix (summary). Full CSV attached (routing-failover-matrix.csv).

title: Geo failover SLO carveout request — Quantum Retail
preview:
From: Priya Shah <priya@quantumretail.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@quantumretail.com>, Dan Moore <dan.moore@quantumretail.com>
Date: Wed, 12 May 2027 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Clarify geo-failover SLA / RTO/RPO expectations

Hi Ben —

Quick question as we tighten up procurement: can Redwood confirm the SLA behavior during a full-region outage (e.g., us-east-1)? Specifically we need: 
- Target RTO for automatic failover (our internal target is <= 5 minutes)
- Target RPO (we'd like <= 15 minutes)
- Whether credits are applied for cross-region outages vs single-region degradation

Our security team is asking for the exact language that will go into the contract. If you have an ops diagram showing routing and cache warm-up assumptions that would help.

Thanks — Priya
Priya Shah
Head of Platform Ops, Quantum Retail
priya@quantumretail.com
(555) 555-0123



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Priya Shah <priya@quantumretail.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 12 May 2027 11:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Clarify geo-failover SLA / RTO/RPO expectations

Hi Priya —

Thanks for the clear ask. Short answer up front: we can commit to a cross-region continuity SLO that reflects operational realities, but we cannot guarantee sub-5-minute automatic failover in all scenarios without an implementation-specific runbook and additional reserved capacity.

High-level proposal (attached: redwood_sla-draft.pdf + dr-architecture-diagram.png):
- Regional uptime: 99.95% per-region availability for production endpoints.
- Cross-region failover SLO: target RTO 15 minutes (time to route + warm caches + restore KV state for active sessions).
- Cross-region recovery point: RPO target 30 minutes for non-critical logs; for token-level KV/cache we provide best-effort prefix/KV caching with eventual consistency guarantees.
- Credits: we propose a differentiated credit schedule — regional outage credits for single-region loss; separate credit tier for full-region failover events.

If Quantum requires hard 5-minute RTO / 15-minute RPO for cross-region failover, that is an accelerated SLA posture that will require dedicated warm standby capacity and a higher committed spend (we can show cost delta).

Next steps: I can loop in Marcus (runtime) and Laura (legal) to put contract language together. Also happy to run a tabletop DR exercise with your ops team.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
+1 415 555 0199

Attachments: redwood_sla-draft.pdf (SLA language + credit schedule), dr-architecture-diagram.png


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 13 May 2027 09:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Clarify geo-failover SLA / RTO/RPO expectations

Ben / Monica —

Reviewed Ben's proposed carveout. A few legal/operational edits we should make before sending to Procurement: 
1) Define "region outage" precisely (carrier outage, AZ-level, control plane vs data plane).
2) Spell out the conditions for invoking cross-region credits (must be a service-affecting event determined by Redwood ops).
3) Add a 90-day run-in period for any accelerated RTO commitments and an opt-out clause for extraordinary force majeure.
4) Insert references to our SOC2 report and annual penetration test (I recommend attaching SOC2-report-2026.pdf).

Marcus — can you add a short paragraph describing cache warm-up assumptions and what customers would see during failover (e.g., ephemeral latency spike, 502s for inflight connections vs queued requests)? A one-page ops note will speed Procurement review.

Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwood.com



From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 14 May 2027 15:10:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Clarify geo-failover SLA / RTO/RPO expectations

On Thu, May 13, 2027 at 09:30 AM Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com> wrote:
> Marcus — can you add a short paragraph describing cache warm-up assumptions...

Attached a short ops note and diagram that matches the draft SLA (dr-architecture-diagram.png). Key points for Procurement: 
- During a full-region failover we initiate automated DNS failover + control-plane routing to standby region. DNS TTLs, client-side caching, and active TCP connections mean observable cutover time varies between 30s-12min depending on client behavior.
- We maintain async KV replication with 1-15 minute lag under normal load; worst-case backed-off writes during region-level event can drive RPO up to 30 minutes for non-idempotent operations.
- We provide an optional warmed standby (hot-standby) for customers who want sub-5-minute RTO; this is scoped as an add-on with reserved capacity and health-check requirements.

I'll also add explicit operational triggers to the draft SLA (what constitutes "failing over to standby", who declares it, and how credits are computed).

— Marcus
Marcus Lin
Director, Serving Runtime, Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.com


From: Priya Shah <priya@quantumretail.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Dan Moore <dan.moore@quantumretail.com>, Procurement Team <procurement@quantumretail.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 May 2027 16:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Clarify geo-failover SLA / RTO/RPO expectations

Ben —

Thanks for the clarity and the draft. We're OK accepting the cross-region SLO: RTO 15 minutes / RPO 30 minutes as long as the contract contains: 
- Clear credit schedule for cross-region events (we want a minimum of 20% credit for an event meeting the failover threshold)
- A commitment to run an annual DR tabletop with our ops+Redwood infra (scheduling to be agreed)
- SOC2 evidence (I see SOC2-report-2026.pdf attached) and a short runbook describing what we should expect during failover (Marcus' note looks good).

Our CISO (Dan) wants DocuSign routing for SLA exhibits and a one-page summary that procurement can attach to the PO. Can you route the final exhibit via DocuSign and include the HubSpot deal id so our procurement team can reconcile?

If you can get legal to finalize and push via DocuSign by end of next week, procurement will start the PO.

— Priya
Priya Shah
Head of Platform Ops, Quantum Retail

title: Short pilot: APAC SLA uplift and pricing for add-on hours vs. tiered discount
preview:
From: Ava Rios <ava.rios@skyrun.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Cc: Sam Patel <sam.patel@skyrun.ai>
Date: Tue, 11 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Quick: APAC pilot — engineering hours vs tiered discount and SLA uplift

Hi Ben and Laura,

Thanks again for the demo last week — the team at Skyrun is excited to move from a sandbox to a short production pilot focused on our APAC customers (mainly Singapore and Sydney).

We have two commercial questions before procurement will sign off:
- We need an option to purchase a small bundle of dedicated engineering hours for integration/ops support during the 6-week pilot (separate from the standard support included).
- Alternatively, procurement would prefer a pricing ladder (tiered discount) tied to a short commit (e.g., 4 / 6 / 8 weeks) instead of buying hours upfront.

Related ask: for APAC workloads we need a 99.5% regional SLA and a short data residency attestation — are those add-ons that can be priced into the pilot, or do they require a Private deployment amendment?

Can you send options and ballpark numbers today/tomorrow? We need to share with legal and procurement for a decision meeting on Friday.

Thanks,
Ava

--
Ava Rios
Head of Product
Skyrun AI
ava.rios@skyrun.ai
+61 412 555 018


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Ava Rios <ava.rios@skyrun.ai>, Sam Patel <sam.patel@skyrun.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 May 2027 11:03:00 -0700

Ava — thanks for the clear ask. High-level options below (ballparks included). Happy to tighten once Laura and Marcus confirm operational details.

title: Request: Exec sign-off — 5yr Stability Covenant + Price Protection for Helix Systems
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.ai>
To: Daniel Park <daniel.park@helixsys.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-05-09T09:00:00-07:00
Subject: Request: Exec sign-off — 5yr Stability Covenant + Price Protection for Helix Systems

Hi Daniel,

Thanks again for the productive call earlier. Per our conversation, Helix is evaluating a 5-year committed package (reserved capacity + hosted API for mission-critical workloads). They asked for multi-year pricing protection and clear renewal mechanics — specifically: a price lock for the first 36 months, then a modest uplift cap thereafter, and contractual renewal protection so their unit economics aren’t materially degraded year-over-year.

Summary of the ask: 
- Term: 5 years (annual billing)
- Price lock: 36 months fixed unit pricing
- Uplift cap: max +3% / year after month 36 (cumulative)
- Renewal protection: no unilateral price jumps >5% in any renewal without 60-day notice + right to terminate at renewal if increase exceeds agreed cap
- Commit: $3.6M ARR (annualized) with minimum monthly MRR rhythm (attached model)

Why this matters: Helix is sensitive to long-term operating cost predictability (regulated healthcare workloads). The customer is ready to commit to the volume we need, but they require contractual stability to secure internal approvals.

What I need from you: 
- Exec approval for the non-standard commercial terms above (price lock + uplift cap + renewal protection).
- Any guardrails you want to add (e.g., minimum quarterly price adjustment floor, reporting cadence, SLA exceptions).

Attachments: Helix_MRR_and_commit_model.xlsx, Proposed_Terms_Summary.pdf

If we greenlight, I’ll hand to Legal for contract language (we should include a fallback to a model variant if capacity constrained). Please reply with approve/decline/approve-with-mods.

Thanks,
Aditya Rao
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
aditya_rao@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0198


From: Daniel Park <daniel.park@helixsys.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.ai>
Cc: Helix Procurement <procurement@helixsys.com>
Date: 2027-05-09T11:30:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Request: Exec sign-off — 5yr Stability Covenant + Price Protection for Helix Systems

Aditya,

Appreciate the quick turnaround. The terms you outlined look close to what we need. Two quick asks from procurement/legal side: 
1) Can we set an uplift floor of 1% / year after month 36 (so changes aren’t one-sided downward pressure on Redwood)?
2) We need a defined remedy window: if Redwood requests >5% increase at renewal, Helix gets option to extend existing pricing for an additional 12 months at prior year rates while procurement escalates internally.

We’ve attached our standard procurement worksheet — will fill and return if you confirm the above guardrails are acceptable.

Daniel Park
Director, Cloud Ops — Helix Systems
daniel.park@helixsys.com


From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>

title: Tail latency budgeting & GPU mapping — RFI response
preview:
From: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: infra-ops@acmehealth.com
Date: 2027-05-01T09:22:00-07:00
Subject: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Hi Ava,

Thanks for the quick intake call this morning. Attached is a short extract from our RFP that focuses on availability and tail-latency commitments. Our infra team is especially concerned about P95 vs P99 tail behavior for short prompt workloads (<=32 tokens output) and large context chat sessions (up to 8k tokens).

Specific asks:
- For reserved capacity, can you indicate per-GPU tail latency distributions (P50/P95/P99) for both A100-80GB and H100-80GB under our workload mix (see attachment)?
- How do you map failover to lower-cost GPUs (e.g., L4 / T4 class) while protecting tail latency and correctness?
- Do you have recommended reservation blocks for mixed workloads (bursty short prompts + sustained long-context chat) that minimize wasted reserved hours?

We'd appreciate a short written reply and a workbook we can use to model our reservations.

Best,
Sophie
Sophie Miller
Head of Infra Ops — Acme Health

Attachments: acme_tail_latency_requirements.pdf


From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-01T11:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Sophie —

Thanks for sending this over. I'm looping in Marcus (runtime perf) and Priyom (capacity planning) to provide the numbers and a workbook you can drop your mixes into. Expect the initial write-up by EOD Monday. A few clarifying notes:

- When you say "protecting tail latency and correctness" for fallback GPUs, do you mean strict P99 guarantees or an SLO that allows graceful degradation with a capped number of degraded requests that return a compatible model variant?
- For your short-prompt mix, can you confirm the average batch size and concurrency target (we can model 1:1 concurrency and optimistic batching separately)?

I'll send a meeting invite if that would be helpful, but we'll aim to reply in the thread first.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>, Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-03T14:18:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Hi Sophie —

We ran quick microbenchmarks and modeled two representative profiles from your attachment:

Profile A: short prompts (<=32 output tokens), median input length ~30 tokens, concurrency target 200 qps
Profile B: long-context chat (8k context, 256 token outputs), concurrency target 30 qps

High-level results (single-request, cold KV cache):
- A100-80GB (fp16 / quant-aware kernels): P50 18ms, P95 42ms, P99 95ms for Profile A at 200 qps with continuous batching tuned to 80ms latency budget.
- H100-80GB (bf16): P50 13ms, P95 30ms, P99 68ms for Profile A — H100 shows consistently lower tail due to faster attention kernels and larger L2.
- For Profile B (long-context), both GPUs see higher medians: A100 P50 120ms / P95 260ms / P99 420ms; H100 P50 95ms / P95 210ms / P99 360ms.

Fallbacks and mapping guidance:
- We do not recommend falling back to L4/T4 for latency-sensitive P99 targets on short prompts unless you accept a higher P99 or perform model quantization + distilled variants. L-class GPUs are cost-effective for background batch/embedding workloads but have higher variance for low-latency web requests.
- Preferred strategy: reserve a primary pool on H100 for latency SLOs and maintain a small warm A100 pool as an intermediate tier. Use policy-based routing: route high-priority traffic to H100; non-critical low-cost requests to L4.
- Graceful degradation: automatic model-variant fallback (same-architecture distilled model) plus optional chunked response streaming to hide latency spikes.

Reservation heuristics we recommend (starter plan for your mixed workload):
- H100 pool: size to reach sustained 30 qps for long-context + headroom for 20% burst (estimate 8 H100 nodes).
- A100 pool: cover remaining short-prompt capacity for steady-state 180 qps (estimate 6 A100 nodes), configured with aggressive batching window and prefix caching.
- On-call burst policy: enable 12-hour burst capacity using shared GPU burst credits to handle unexpected spikes without overcommitting reserved hours.

I've attached a blank capacity workbook you can use to plug in your exact qps, concurrency, and percent-burst assumptions. The workbook includes sheets for kernel mix, per-model token cost, and tail-latency budget allocation.

Happy to walk through numbers live.

Regards,
Marcus Lin
Senior Runtime Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood_dedicated_capacity_workbook.xlsx


From: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: infra-ops@acmehealth.com
Date: 2027-05-06T08:47:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Thanks Marcus — this is very helpful. A couple follow-ups after reviewing the workbook: 

1) For the H100 pool you recommend "8 nodes" for long-context. Can you surface the utilization threshold you used to size that? Is that 70% GPU util or 50%? We need to map to our SLA burn-rate for reserved hours.
2) If we enable auto-burst credits for 12 hours, how do you meter and bill that? Is burst billed at reserved rate or on-demand?
3) We're curious about tail-latency isolation: if one tenant in our multi-tenant namespace floods the prefix cache and causes KV miss storms, how does Redwood surface/limit that to meet P99 for others?

Would appreciate short answers inline and we can reconvene if needed.

Best,
Sophie


From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Miller <sophie@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-05-09T16:38:00-07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: tail-latency targets and GPU mapping for inference reservations

Sophie — quick inline replies and a couple of examples from the workbook.

1) Sizing utilization assumption: we sized H100 nodes to an average target of ~60% sustained GPU util for long-context sessions. That gives ~25% headroom for KV-cache churn and unexpected attention kernel variance while keeping reserved-hour efficiency reasonable. You can change the sheet to 50%/70% to see cost impact; the workbook has those toggles.

2) Billing for burst credits: burst is billed at the committed reserved rate up to your committed capacity; additional burst beyond your committed burst allowance is billed at on-demand pricing. The workbook's "Billing" tab models 12-hour daily burst allowance vs ad-hoc burst, and outputs monthly cost estimates. We also include an example contract clause for enterprise agreements to cap burst overage.

3) Tail-latency isolation & KV storms: engineering controls we deploy:
   - Per-tenant prefix cache quotas and eviction priorities; if a tenant triggers a high miss rate we'll throttle that tenant's new prefix caching for a short recovery window.
   - Request-level QoS: mark SLO-critical requests and route them to isolated worker pools with reserved KV cache regions.
   - Real-time alerts: when miss rate for a tenant exceeds threshold, we emit an auto page to infra-ops and move the tenant to a protected pool until manual review.

Attached is a filled example scenario using your sample traffic numbers. If you want, we can run your real traffic trace in our sandbox to produce a drop-in sizing recommendation.

Next steps:
- I'll share the completed workbook with sample toggles and a short 30-minute walkthrough (optional).
- Ava, can you coordinate contracting touchpoints if Acme wants to pursue a Dedicated commitment?

Thanks,
Priyom Das
Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood_dedicated_capacity_workbook.xlsx (scenario example)
